title: Gate on fast-tier p99 and standard-tier p95
preview: Summary: This PR adds tier-aware SLO gating to perf-canary for the Workload-aware Batching Defaults launch. We now fail the perf-canary run if: (1) FAST tier p99 latency regresses beyond threshold (tail-latency protection gate), or (2) STANDARD tier p95 latency regresses beyond threshold (steady-state gate). The intent is to stop merges that silently violate the published tier SLOs while still allowing legitimate improvements to land with a controlled baseline refresh process. Motivation: As we move batching config from ad-hoc per-route knobs to three customer-facing tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient), we need CI guardrails that match what we’ll ship. Previously, canary gating was based on a single aggregate p95 and a single scenario per model. That missed fast-tier tail regressions (p99) and could overreact to noise in standard tier when concurrency ramps changed. What changed (high level): (1) Adds a tier_slo_gates.yaml config describing which percentile to gate on per tier, thresholds, and the scenario → tier mapping. (2) Extends the results parser to ingest tier labels from benchmark-lab output (scenario metadata), and compute per-tier regression deltas against the stored baseline. (3) Adds a baseline refresh workflow with explicit approvals, including a short “baseline justification” note that is stored next to the refreshed baseline file. (4) Adds a regression triage runbook tailored for tiered batching: how to distinguish scheduler regressions vs workload-mix drift, and what artifacts to attach to the Linear issue. Details: Gating rules: FAST tier: gate on p99 end-to-end latency, primary metric = request_latency_ms (includes queue + prefill + decode), scenario group = mixed_workload_streaming_shortprefill + mixed_workload_nonstreaming_shortprefill. Threshold default = +6% relative regression OR +25ms absolute (whichever triggers first) with a minimum sample size requirement. STANDARD tier: gate on p95 end-to-end latency, scenario group = mixed_workload_balanced + summarization_mediumprefill. Threshold default = +5% relative regression OR +35ms absolute. Note: COST-EFFICIENT tier is intentionally not a merge-blocking gate in this PR; we still compute and report it, but it only posts a warning annotation. This matches the staged rollout plan and avoids blocking on known variance for aggressive batching experiments. Noise controls: (a) sample-size gate: if we have < N requests for a tier/scenario, the gate is “insufficient data” and does not fail the run (but posts a warning). (b) warm-up exclusion: ignore first K requests in each scenario to reduce cold-start variance. (c) optional “max allowed stddev” heuristic (disabled by default) included behind a flag for future tuning. Baseline refresh process (new): We now store baselines under baselines/<model_family>/<hardware_class>/baseline.json and include a matching baselines/<...>/baseline.meta.txt with: timestamp, perf-canary run URL, benchmark-lab revision, and a one-paragraph human summary of why the refresh is safe (e.g., “intentional latency improvement due to scheduler tweak”). To refresh: run ./scripts/refresh_baseline.sh --model-family llama --hw h100 --scenario-set tiers --run-id <canary-run> then open a PR containing only baseline files + meta. That PR must have approvals from one Runtime reviewer and one SRE reviewer. Regression triage notes (new docs): Added docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md. Key guidance: verify the tier label in the artifact, check queue depth & batch-size distribution in the attached benchmark-lab plots, compare streaming vs non-streaming deltas (fast-tier p99 is usually driven by admission/wait budget), and tag the resulting Linear issue with runtime+slo-gate. Also includes “when to refresh baseline” vs “when to block merge and fix” decision table. Changed areas/files (high level): - config/tier_slo_gates.yaml: new canonical gating config - src/gating/tier_slo_gate.py: new tier-aware gate implementation - src/results/parser.py: scenario metadata → tier mapping + percentile extraction - src/cli.py: new flags for tier gating, warm-up exclusion - scripts/refresh_baseline.sh: baseline refresh helper + guardrails - docs/baseline_refresh.md: refresh procedure + required approvals - docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md: triage playbook - .github/workflows/perf-canary.yml: upload tier gate summary, annotate PRs Commit summary: 1) parser: ingest scenario metadata and emit tier breakdown 2) gating: implement fast p99 + standard p95 gates w/ thresholds 3) scripts/docs: add baseline refresh + triage docs 4) workflow: upload artifacts + PR annotations Validation: - Ran on 6 recent canary runs for top model families; no false failures after warm-up exclusion and sample-size checks - Verified that a known fast-tier p99 regression (synthetic +12% added wait budget) fails the gate - Verified that a known standard-tier improvement (scheduler prefill/decode split change) passes and baseline can be refreshed cleanly Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Add an optional gate for COST-EFFICIENT tier once variance is reduced (likely after we stabilize queue backpressure heuristics) - Integrate “reason codes” (profile selection telemetry) into the canary report when available from runtime 1.22 - Expand coverage to additional regions/hardware classes once Dedicated fleet baselines exist

title: Add auto-rollback dashboards + alert rules (triggered/suppressed/stale monitors) for rollout safety
preview: Context: Redwood rollouts now evaluate SLO signals (latency/error burn-rate + staleness) and can auto-pause/auto-rollback. We need first-class operator visibility and sane alerts before enabling this broadly in production. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and Prometheus alert rules for auto-rollback outcomes (triggered/paused/suppressed), monitor coverage & staleness, and a per-service rollout health view. Dashboards: (1) Rollout Safety — Overview: shows decision volume and rate, breakdown by service/route/model_version/region/tier, and top suppress reasons; includes a panel for “monitor missing/stale” to catch the most common gap from game day/postmortems. (2) Rollout Safety — Rollout Detail: scoped to rollout_id; shows decision timeline + links to rollout controller logs and SLO monitor ids (via labels). (3) Rollout Safety — Service Health: operators pick service + route; shows request volume, p95/p99, error rate, burn-rate status (fast/slow windows), and decision outcomes overlaid. Alerts: (A) RedwoodAutoRollbackTriggered (severity=page) fires when rollback is executed for a production tier and not suppressed; pages SRE and notifies service channel (routing via labels: service/team). (B) RedwoodAutoRollbackPaused (severity=notify) for auto-pause outcomes (default notify only, per PM MVP). (C) RedwoodAutoRollbackSuppressed (severity=notify) for suppress actions (admin override / suppression window) so oncall can validate intent and duration. (D) RolloutSLOMonitorStale (severity=notify) when the monitor adapter reports stale data for the rollout scope beyond threshold (default 10m) OR the evaluation pipeline hasn’t produced fresh samples; includes staleness reason label (telemetry_lag|adapter_error|no_samples). (E) RolloutSLOMonitorMissing (severity=notify) when a rollout is configured for SLO-driven actions but no eligible monitor exists for the scope (route/model_version/region/tier) for >15m. Notes/guardrails: uses low-cardinality labels only; rollout_id is only used in dashboards (templated via ad-hoc filters) and is NOT used in alert grouping keys to avoid paging storms. Alerts group by service, route, region, customer_tier. Also adds a short README section describing routing + how to validate monitors before enabling auto-rollback (ties to release gating work). Follow-ups: once PR-477 in incident-bot is live everywhere, we can switch the notify-only alerts to Slack-only with deep links; for now we keep Prometheus alerts as the primary signal for oncall and use Slack notifications as secondary.

title: Post rollout auto-rollback events to Slack (concise notification + links)
preview: Summary: incident-bot now posts a Slack notification when the rollout controller triggers an automatic safety action due to an SLO breach (auto_rollback, auto_pause, or rollback_suppressed). The message is intentionally short, includes the rollout_id and scope, and provides deep links to (1) the rollout in Console, (2) the rollout SLO dashboard, and (3) the audit event details. Motivation: during the 2026-02 simulations and recent customer escalations, oncall often learned about auto-mitigations only after secondary alerts or customer reports; this closes the loop and reduces time-to-context while keeping noise bounded. Event source: consumes the new structured audit event emitted to the event stream (redwood PR-28435) with event_type=rollout.auto_action.*. The handler maps event subtypes to Slack severity and routing without paging by default (notify-only) to avoid paging storms. Message format (example): "[AUTO-ROLLBACK] rollout=rol_01HT… scope=route:/v1/chat model=llama3.1-70b-v3 region=us-east tier=dedicated reason=p99_latency_burnrate_fast" followed by links: Console rollout, Grafana dashboard, Audit event (internal). Guardrails: (a) dedupe by (rollout_id, action, step_id) for 10 minutes, (b) drop messages if required links can’t be constructed (still increments a metric), (c) redacts customer identifiers (uses customer_tier + optional org_id hash only), (d) rate limits per channel to 1 msg/sec with burst=5. Routing: auto_rollback -> #eng-oncall (notify) + #eng-sre (notify); auto_pause -> #eng-platform (notify); rollback_suppressed -> thread reply on the most recent auto action message if present, otherwise #eng-sre (notify). Configuration: adds rollout_auto_actions.enabled (default false in prod; true in staging), rollout_auto_actions.channels overrides, and a link template block for Console/Grafana base URLs per environment. Observability: emits incident_bot_slack_posts_total{type=rollout_auto_action,outcome=sent|dropped|rate_limited|deduped} and incident_bot_rollout_auto_action_events_total{action=rollback|pause|suppressed}. Testing: unit tests cover payload parsing, formatting, dedupe, and redaction; an integration test uses a fixture event to validate generated Slack blocks; updated run instructions in README. Rollout plan: staging enablement first (paired with redwood PR-28490), then prod behind flag once dashboards/alerts are confirmed (observability-pack PR-913).

title: Add Azure Sentinel (Log Analytics) export target + Private deployment config examples
preview:
Context
We’re standardizing audit event schema v1 across Hosted/Dedicated/Private and expanding supported SIEM export targets. This PR adds an Azure Sentinel / Log Analytics sink to audit-log-exporter using the Azure Monitor Logs Ingestion API (DCE + DCR) and includes configuration examples for Private deployments.

Why
- Multiple enterprise/security reviews (and SUP-18477) requested first-class Sentinel support.
- Private customers want a supported path that doesn’t rely on “roll your own” forwarders.
- We need consistent payload validation + schema_version tagging before exporting to third-party systems (ties to ENG-4827).

What’s in this PR
1) New sink: azure_sentinel
- Adds a new output target: type=azure_sentinel
- Uses Azure AD OAuth2 client credentials to obtain an access token.
- Posts batches to Azure Monitor Logs Ingestion endpoint (DCE) for a configured Data Collection Rule (DCR) + stream.
- Defaults to emitting JSON as-is (after v1 normalization) and relies on DCR transformation to map into a custom table, with guidance on recommended column mapping.

2) Request shaping + delivery semantics
- Batching:
  - Max events per request: configurable (default 500)
  - Max payload bytes: configurable (default 900KB to stay under typical proxy limits; Azure supports larger, but we keep headroom)
- Retries:
  - Retry on 429/5xx and network errors
  - Exponential backoff with jitter
  - Honors Retry-After when present
- Idempotency guidance:
  - Azure ingestion API doesn’t provide a built-in idempotency key.
  - We include event_id and recommend downstream de-dupe by (event_id, tenant_id) (ties to SUP-18510).

3) Validation + redaction
- Reuses existing schema_version tagging and v1 normalization pipeline.
- Ensures we do not log exporter secrets (client_secret, shared keys) and masks token values in debug logs.
- Adds a small allow-list for high-cardinality fields in exporter metrics to avoid cardinality explosions.

4) Private deployment documentation/examples
- Adds docs snippets for:
  - Helm values.yaml configuration
  - Environment variable configuration
  - Example Kubernetes Secret manifest
  - Troubleshooting checklist (401/403, 429 throttling, DCR mismatch)
- Notes on Azure prerequisites:
  - DCE endpoint URL
  - DCR immutable ID
  - Stream name
  - Service principal credentials (tenant_id, client_id, client_secret)
  - Required permissions (scope to the DCR/DCE resource per Azure guidance)

Example configuration (Helm values)
exporter:
  sinks:
    - type: azure_sentinel
      name: sentinel-prod
      enabled: true
      azure:
        tenant_id: ${AZURE_TENANT_ID}
        client_id: ${AZURE_CLIENT_ID}
        client_secret: ${AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET}
        dce_endpoint: https://<dce-name>.<region>.ingest.monitor.azure.com
        dcr_immutable_id: dcr-00000000000000000000000000000000
        stream_name: Custom-RedwoodAuditV1
      batching:
        max_events: 500
        max_bytes: 900000
        flush_interval_ms: 1000
      retry:
        max_attempts: 12
        base_backoff_ms: 250
        max_backoff_ms: 30000

Implementation notes
- New package: sinks/azure_sentinel
  - token provider with caching (refreshes before expiry)
  - ingestion client with typed errors and retry classification
- Adds exporter metrics:
  - exporter_sink_requests_total{sink="azure_sentinel",status="success|fail"}
  - exporter_sink_request_latency_ms_bucket{sink="azure_sentinel"}
  - exporter_sink_dropped_events_total{sink="azure_sentinel",reason="oversize|validation|permanent_error"}
- Adds unit tests:
  - token caching + refresh logic
  - Retry-After handling
  - payload size capping behavior
  - redaction/masking in logs
- Adds an opt-in integration test (skipped by default) that can be run in CI with Azure credentials (documented in CONTRIBUTING).

Files/areas changed (high level)
- src/sinks/azure_sentinel/* (new)
- src/config/* (new sink config schema + validation)
- src/pipeline/* (wiring sink into existing normalization/validation flow)
- docs/sinks/azure_sentinel.md (new)
- deploy/private/examples/azure-sentinel-values.yaml (new)
- deploy/private/examples/azure-sentinel-secret.yaml (new)
- CONTRIBUTING.md (integration test instructions)

Review discussion highlights
- Hanae: requested explicit masking of client_secret and access tokens in logs; added logger scrubber + tests.
- Allison: asked to keep metrics labels low-cardinality; removed per-stream labels and kept sink-level only.
- Sean: requested clearer behavior when Azure endpoint is down; updated sink to classify failures and avoid hot loops; added runbook notes in docs.
- Nadia: asked to confirm schema_version is included and that we don’t mutate reserved fields; confirmed normalization happens before sink and added a regression test verifying schema_version propagation.
- Amina: requested Private examples include Kubernetes Secret + note on rotating credentials; added both.

CI
- build: pass
- unit tests: pass
- lint: pass
- gosec/staticcheck: pass
- integration tests: skipped (requires Azure creds)

Rollout plan
- Ship behind config (new sink type only becomes active when configured).
- Early validation with one Private customer environment via Solutions Eng (tracked in ENG-4836).
- Follow-up: add Terraform snippet for DCR/DCE provisioning in docs (separate PR to keep this focused).

Merge
Squash merged to main.

title: Add Suggestions API + funnel Grafana dashboards and beta alerting
preview: Summary: Adds a first-pass set of Grafana dashboards + alert rules for the Optimization Suggestions beta, covering (1) Suggestions API health (latency/errors/payload) and (2) the Suggestions view funnel (impression → expand → dismiss → apply-click), including breakdowns by org/model/route/region with conservative cardinality controls. Motivation: during dogfood we had a p95 regression + early customer reports of “empty despite traffic” and “estimates look off”; we need a single place for SRE/Platform + product to validate the pipeline (compute freshness), the API (error/latency), and the UX funnel (are people seeing/using suggestions). Scope notes: This PR only adds dashboards/alerts in observability-pack; it assumes the backend/UI already emits the metrics/events (ENG-19402) and the Suggestions API exists (ENG-19341). No changes to application code here. Dashboards added: (A) Optimize / Suggestions API Health: request rate, p50/p95 latency, 4xx/5xx, top error codes, payload size, cache hit/miss (if available), and per-route hot spots. Includes links to the runbook for triage. (B) Optimize / Suggestions Funnel (Beta): counts + conversion rates for suggestions_impression, suggestions_row_expand, suggestions_dismiss, suggestions_apply_click; time series + cohort table, with optional filters (org_id, project_id, model, route, region) and a “minimum volume” guardrail. (C) Optimize / Suggestions Compute Freshness: staleness/lag from the compute job (stale_as_of age), recompute success/failure, and lag percentiles so support can tell if “insufficient telemetry” is real vs pipeline delay. Alerting: Adds lightweight beta alert rules with conservative thresholds and paging only on clear system issues. SuggestionsAPIHigh5xxRate (page): 5xx > 1% for 10m (per env) with a minimum request volume gate. SuggestionsAPIP95LatencyHigh (page): p95 > 350ms for 15m (same volume gate). SuggestionsComputeLagHigh (page): compute freshness lag p95 > 6h for 30m. SuggestionsEventIngestionDrop (ticket): impressions drop to ~0 while API request volume is non-zero (helps catch client-side event pipeline breaks). All alerts include runbook links and labels for routing to eng-sre/eng-platform. Privacy/cardinality: Funnel breakdowns use org_id/project_id (numeric/UUID) and do not include org name; we cap table panels to top-N by volume and add a $__min_volume variable to reduce noisy / low-signal slices. Notes for rollout: Dashboards are safe to merge before the beta flag expands; alerts should be enabled in staging immediately and in prod with “ticket” severity for 24h before paging (see checklist).

title: Audit log pipeline dashboards + alerts (ingestion latency, drop rate) with runbook links
preview:
Context: As part of the audit logging standardization effort we’re tightening operational visibility for the audit event ingestion/export path. We’ve had a couple of near-misses where ingestion lag spiked without a clear on-call landing page, and early alert drafts lacked enough context to triage quickly.

This PR adds:
- A Grafana dashboard for the audit-log ingestion pipeline (end-to-end delivery latency, ingest backlog, drop rate, retry/429/5xx, DLQ growth).
- Alert rules for delivery latency and drop rate with sane defaults + per-environment routing.
- Runbook links embedded in alert annotations and dashboard panel descriptions.

Key signals / definitions:
- delivery_latency_seconds: time from event emission at producer to ack at ingestion sink (measured via audit-ingest gateway metrics + exporter timestamps).
- drop_rate: fraction of events not successfully delivered after retry budget (from ingest and exporter counters).
- backlog: queued events in buffer (when enabled) + ingestion consumer lag.

Alert thresholds (initial):
- AuditLogDeliveryLatencyP99High: p99(delivery_latency_seconds) > 60s for 10m (page: enterprise tiers; ticket: others).
- AuditLogDropRateHigh: drop_rate > 0.10% for 5m (page; includes per-region breakdown).
- AuditLogIngestBacklogGrowing: backlog > 50k AND increasing for 15m (ticket; pages if > 200k).

Cardinality guardrails:
- Dashboard panels group primarily by region, deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private), and service. We intentionally do not group by raw tenant_id/project_id. event_type is only used in a single table panel with top-N + allow-listed types.

Runbooks:
- Incident runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/audit-log-pipeline-incident-runbook.json
- Coverage regression runbook is linked from the dashboard “Coverage” row for follow-up when events are missing but pipeline is healthy.

Testing:
- jsonnetfmt + dashboards render in CI.
- promtool rules tests added for the new alert expressions.

Notes:
- This is intended to be the “default landing page” linked from paging alerts. We can iterate thresholds after a week of production data; see INT-3984 for noise tuning follow-ups.

title: Quality gates: dashboards + alert rules (pass rate, failure spikes, latency, flake rate)
preview: Adds first-class observability for Optimize quality gate runs. This PR introduces (1) a Grafana dashboard for the full gate-run funnel (created → running → passed/failed/overridden), (2) Prometheus alert rules for gate failure spikes, flake rate regressions, and gate execution latency/SLO breaches, and (3) runbook links + consistent labels so we can slice by config_type (batching/cache/quant_profile), model_family, route_id, account_tier, and region. Motivation: we’re rolling out mandatory gating ahead of applying Optimize recommendations; we need to catch (a) real quality regressions, (b) eval-harness instability/flaky suites causing false blocks, and (c) infrastructure constraints causing timeouts (notably EU-West). Design notes: alerts avoid paging on single-run failures; they trigger on burn-rate / sustained increases and include a ‘possible flake’ annotation when rerun aggregation indicates high variance. Dashboard includes deep links back into Console (gate run id) and the gate failure runbook for oncall. This aligns metric names/tags with the quality-gates observability standard and uses the new gate_run_id trace correlation field emitted by the Optimize service. Rollout: rules are initially deployed in ‘warning’ severity for 1 week; paging thresholds are enabled after we confirm baseline noise levels in limited production rollout.

title: Monitor adapter: query latency + error SLO burn-rates (with staleness + sample sizes)
preview:
Context: Auto-rollback on SLO breach needs the rollout controller to fetch burn-rate status for the exact rollout scope (route/model_version/region/customer_tier) and make decisions with guardrails (sample size + staleness handling). This PR adds a monitor adapter in `redwood` that queries the internal slo-toolkit and normalizes results for the rollout controller.

What’s in this PR:
- New `SloMonitorAdapter` interface + `SloToolkitAdapter` implementation.
- Fetches burn-rate status for latency (p95/p99) and error-rate SLOs, returning both fast/slow windows (e.g., 5m/30m) and per-window sample sizes.
- Staleness detection:
  - Treats a monitor result as STALE when `now - data_timestamp > staleness_threshold` (default 2m; configurable).
  - Treats missing timestamps or NaN burn-rate as UNKNOWN (distinct from OK/BREACH).
- Resilience:
  - Timeouts + bounded retries when calling slo-toolkit.
  - Small in-process cache (TTL 10s) keyed by (scope + slo_type + objective) to avoid hammering slo-toolkit during rollouts.
  - Explicit UNAVAILABLE state when toolkit is down; includes error string for auditability.
- Adds adapter-level metrics (low-cardinality) for call volume/errors/staleness/latency.
- Tests: unit tests for staleness + parsing + caching behavior; integration test with a fake slo-toolkit server.

Notes / follow-ups:
- The rollout controller will still apply its own minimum-sample gating + debounce (ENG-4861 / PR-28463). This adapter returns sample sizes and timestamps so the controller can decide fail-open vs fail-closed per policy.
- slo-toolkit endpoint used here expects rollout-scoped labels; the corresponding toolkit work is in slo-toolkit PR-221.

Screenshots: N/A (backend-only).

Risk: Moderate. Adapter is behind `rollouts.slo_adapter.enabled` and is not invoked unless the rollout policy enables SLO-based actions.

Rollout plan:
- Merge behind flag.
- Enable in staging for internal canary route `chat/completions` (internal tenant only).
- Observe adapter error rate + staleness metrics for 48h before expanding.

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration test with fake toolkit
- [x] Metrics added (cardinality reviewed)
- [x] Flag default OFF
- [x] Docs/commented example response payload

title: Add SDK retry/timeout/streaming disconnect dashboard panels + rollout alerts
preview:
Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity rollout (standard retries/backoff, timeout model, and streaming disconnect handling), SRE requested first-class dashboards and conservative alerts to monitor load impact and customer-facing failure rates during beta/stable releases. This PR adds (1) new panels to the existing "SDK Client Health" Grafana dashboard for retry rate, timeout rate, and streaming disconnect reasons, (2) companion alert rules with rollout-friendly thresholds and burn-rate style paging, and (3) a small set of recording rules to make the queries cheaper/consistent across environments.

What’s included: - Dashboard panels: Retry rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, model, status_code), Retry attempts distribution (p50/p95 of retry_count per request), Timeout rate (by sdk_language, timeout_scope=overall|connect|read), Timeout duration (histogram heatmap where available), Streaming disconnect rate (by sdk_language, endpoint, reason), Top streaming disconnect reasons (table), and a rollout view grouped by sdk_version (when the label is present). - Alerts (disabled-by-default in prod, enabled in beta + dogfood): (A) High SDK retry rate (warn), (B) High SDK timeout rate (warn), (C) Streaming disconnect spike (warn), (D) Potential retry storm indicator (page) using (retry_total / request_total) + 429 rate + concurrency as gates. - Recording rules: pre-aggregate sdk_client_request_total, sdk_client_retry_total, sdk_client_timeout_total, sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total into 5m rate series with a bounded label set.

Metrics used (existing / recently standardized fields): - sdk_client_request_total{sdk_language, sdk_version?, endpoint, model, status_code} - sdk_client_retry_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_timeout_total{sdk_language, endpoint, timeout_scope} - sdk_client_stream_disconnect_total{sdk_language, endpoint, reason} - sdk_client_request_duration_seconds_bucket (optional; used when emitted)

Notes / assumptions: - Streaming disconnect reasons follow the parity taxonomy being rolled out in SDKs (e.g., server_fin, read_timeout, client_cancel, proxy_reset, tls_error, unknown). Panels will still render if reason is missing (falls into reason="unknown" in the dashboards). - To avoid alert noise from a small number of clients, alerts include a minimum volume gate (requests_per_min) and are scoped to the rollout dashboards folder. - This PR intentionally does NOT add new instrumentation; it only consumes the standardized fields being added in the SDK repos. If a given SDK release doesn’t yet include sdk_version label, the dashboard will still work (the sdk_version view will simply be empty).

Rollout plan: - Merge now; ship to dogfood + beta monitoring stacks. - Enable alerts in beta immediately; keep prod alerts muted until stable releases are out (target: after ENG-5131 go/no-go). - During the first week of stable: watch retry_rate, timeout_rate, and disconnect_reason mix for proxy_reset/read_timeout regressions; use request_id sampling from SDK logs to correlate.

Testing: - Rendered dashboards via jsonnet/grafonnet compile locally; validated queries against staging Prometheus with synthetic load. - Ran alert rule unit tests (promtool) with fixtures covering 429 spikes and elevated read timeouts.

Screenshots: Included in the PR conversation (Grafana panels: Retry rate by language, Streaming disconnect reasons table).

Follow-ups: - Add a dedicated panel for Retry-After compliance once the gateway header adoption is >90% (separate PR). - Consider a dashboard annotation hook for SDK release cut times (Connor coordinating in ENG-RELEASES).

title: Add Quality Gate enforcement check stage to perf-canary pipeline
preview:
Background: Optimize now requires quality gates before applying cost-saving config diffs (batching/cache/quantization). We’ve had a near-miss during beta where enforcement drifted between services (Optimize apply endpoint blocked correctly, rollout promotion didn’t). This PR adds a dedicated perf-canary stage that continuously verifies gate enforcement semantics end-to-end against a controlled staging route, and alerts on regressions.

What this adds:
- New canary stage: `quality_gate_enforcement`
  - Executes an end-to-end scenario against staging Optimize + eval-harness + rollout-controller API surface.
  - Validates enforcement at the apply boundary (blocked when gate != PASSED) and at promotion boundary (canary promotion blocked on failed gate).
- A small, deterministic test fixture:
  - Uses a dedicated canary account + route (non-production) and a synthetic config diff that is expected to FAIL the gate (known-bad quant profile on long-context prompts).
  - Uses a separate config diff that is expected to PASS smoke gates (benign batching window tweak) to ensure the stage itself isn’t only testing the fail path.
- Result reporting:
  - Emits `perf_canary_stage_result{stage="quality_gate_enforcement"}` and per-assertion tags (apply_blocked, promotion_blocked, pass_path_ok).
  - Posts a structured summary to #eng-sre-canaries when failures persist > 2 runs, with links to gate run ids and artifacts.

Behavior / Failure modes:
- Stage is non-flaky by design: retries only on 5xx/timeouts; no retries on assertion failures.
- Hard timeout: 12m for the stage (to avoid blocking the entire canary suite). If evals exceed budget we treat as failure and page as SLO regression (this catches the "gates blocking rollouts too long" class of issues).
- Guardrails: stage aborts early if canary environment is not "staging" or if the route/account id doesn’t match the allowlist.

Rollout:
- Enabled in staging canary pipeline immediately.
- For prod canary pipeline, staged behind `CANARY_ENABLE_QUALITY_GATE_STAGE=true` (default off) so SRE can enable after one week of stable staging signal.

Notes:
- This PR does not change gating logic; it only validates existing behavior and surfaces regressions earlier.
- Related: ENG-3928 (rollout controller respects gate), ENG-3926 (metrics), ENG-3931 (required checks).

Screenshots/Artifacts:
- Example success run output is included in the PR comments (log excerpt + metric names).

title: Fix rate-limit/quota header consistency across endpoints + add regression tests
preview:
Context: Support has multiple reports of confusing/incorrect rate-limit headers after SDK upgrades and during fallback routing (SUP-1843, SUP-1855). In particular: (1) some endpoints emitted RateLimit-* while others only emitted X-RateLimit-*; (2) reset values were inconsistently rounded (ms vs s); (3) quota-limit vs request-rate-limit were conflated, resulting in 429 responses with headers that suggested a long-term quota exhaustion when the user was simply bursting; (4) in a subset of fallback codepaths, we emitted headers computed from the upstream pool defaults instead of the customer/account plan, causing Remaining to jump between requests. This PR standardizes header emission across all public inference endpoints, splits "rate limit" vs "quota" semantics, and adds regression coverage for gateway + fallback paths.

What’s included: - Header standardization: Always emit IETF-style RateLimit-Limit, RateLimit-Remaining, RateLimit-Reset (seconds) on 200/4xx responses for chat/completions, embeddings, rerank, and legacy completions routes. - Back-compat: Continue emitting X-RateLimit-Limit/Remaining/Reset for now (same values). Added a single codepath that computes both sets, so they can’t diverge. - Quota vs rate: Introduce Redwood-Quota-Limit and Redwood-Quota-Remaining (tokens/day for hosted plans; capacity units for Dedicated where applicable). These are intentionally separate from RateLimit-* (requests/min and tokens/min), and are omitted when unknown/unavailable (e.g., Private deployments with customer-managed policy). - 429 classification: Ensure the JSON error payload includes error.type in {"rate_limit_exceeded","quota_exceeded"} and set Retry-After consistently for rate-limit 429s. - Fallback path fix: When routing falls back to a compatible model/region, headers now reflect the customer/account limits (not the fallback pool defaults). Also adds a counter metric redwood.gateway.ratelimit.header_source{source=account|pool_default} to help debug mismatches.

Out of scope: changes to pricing/entitlements, UI changes in Console, or SDK behavior changes beyond consuming these headers.

Rollout notes: This is safe to ship as it’s additive. Existing headers are preserved with identical values. We can deprecate X-RateLimit-* once clients confirm RateLimit-* adoption (tracking in ENG-4825).

title: Add audit log lag dashboard + alerts and wire runbook links (ENG-4824)
preview:
Context: Support + CS escalations (notably around audit log delivery latency) highlighted that we lacked a first-class, Support/CS-friendly view of end-to-end audit log pipeline lag and an alert that aligns to enterprise expectations. This PR adds a dedicated dashboard and alerting rules for audit log lag, and ensures panels/alerts link to a canonical runbook. It is intended to reduce time-to-triage during tickets like SUP-1846 and to support the known-issues sprint playbook work by providing an authoritative signal.

What’s included: (1) New Grafana dashboard: "Audit Logs — Delivery Lag" with panels for ingestion → transform → delivery stages, p50/p95/p99 lag, backlog depth (where available), per-region breakdown, and a quick "is this customer-impacting" section (current lag vs expected SLO). (2) New Prometheus alerts: audit-log-lag warning + critical, scoped to production namespaces and grouped by region, with annotations that link directly to the runbook and dashboard. (3) Runbook updates: new section for audit log lag triage, plus explicit "Support-facing guidance" (what we can safely confirm, what data we should avoid sharing, and what mitigation knobs exist). (4) Routing/ownership: alerts are routed to SRE on-call; annotations include escalation guidance for Platform ownership boundaries.

Alert thresholds (aligned to enterprise expectations): - Warning: p95 delivery lag > 10m for 10m (per region) - Critical: p95 delivery lag > 30m for 10m (per region) Notes: These thresholds intentionally do not page on transient spikes; they aim to catch sustained backlog that will breach enterprise expectations. Runbook describes how to validate whether this is isolated to a subset of tenants vs global/regional.

Key design choices: - Use p95 as the primary paging signal to avoid single-tenant tail behavior dominating. Dashboard still shows p99 for investigation. - Region grouping matches how Support/CS communicate impact (us-east/eu-west/etc.) and how status events are typically framed. - Runbook contains customer-safe language; no tenant identifiers or customer names are present in dashboard queries.

Testing/validation: - Verified dashboard JSON imports cleanly into staging Grafana and renders for both "prod" and "staging" datasources. - Verified alert rule evaluation against a replayed backlog scenario (staging) triggers warning/critical as expected and resolves cleanly. - Verified links in alert annotations resolve (runbook, dashboard, and on-call channel).

Rollout: - Merge to main + apply via standard observability-pack deploy pipeline (next scheduled deploy window). - SRE on-call to monitor for noisy alerts during first 48 hours; if noisy, adjust the for-duration before adjusting thresholds.

Screenshots: Included in PR checks artifacts (Grafana render export) for quick review.

Follow-ups (out of scope for this PR): - Add a dedicated metric for per-tenant lag that can be surfaced only in restricted dashboards (requires security review). - Add an explicit "expected delivery SLO" panel driven by tier metadata (would require joining tier labels into metrics).

title: Make quality gating policy configurable per-route and account tier
preview:
Title: Make quality gating policy configurable per-route and account tier

Context
Today, quality gates are effectively “one size fits all” once the feature flag is enabled. This creates friction for:
- Self-serve routes where we want a fast smoke gate by default (DX) and escalated full gates for higher-risk diffs.
- Enterprise / regulated routes where we want stricter defaults (full gates always, tighter override rules) and explicit auditability.

This PR introduces a first-class gating policy object that can be resolved by (route_id, account_tier, model_family) with sane defaults. Enforcement remains defense-in-depth (Optimize apply boundary + rollout promotion), but now reads a consistent policy source.

What’s in this PR
1) Policy model + resolution
- Adds GatePolicy and GatePolicyBinding data model.
- Resolution order:
  1. Route override (route_id)
  2. Tier default (account_tier)
  3. Global default
- Optional predicates for model_family and change_type (batching/cache/quant_profile).

2) Default policies (MVP)
- global_default: smoke gate required for apply; full gate required for canary promotion.
- tier:self_serve: smoke required; full required only for quant_profile changes.
- tier:enterprise: full required for all in-scope change types.

3) Enforcement wiring
- Optimize ApplyConfig endpoint now loads effective policy and enforces required gate tier(s).
- Rollout controller promotion check reads the same effective policy for the route/tier.
- Override flow unchanged but now validates against policy (e.g., enterprise requires explicit approver role).

4) Console/Optimize API surface
- Gate run summary endpoints now include "effective_policy" metadata (id + human-readable summary + resolved_from).
- Allows UI to explain “why you’re blocked” and what gate tier is required.

5) Migration/backfill
- Introduces a migration to create global_default and tier defaults.
- Backfills existing gating-enabled routes to a route binding matching the prior behavior to avoid surprise changes.

Out of scope
- Private/on-prem automation policy distribution (this will be design-only for now).
- Per-account custom policies beyond tier (we can add account_id bindings later if needed).

Testing
- Unit tests for policy resolution ordering and fallbacks.
- Integration tests for apply boundary enforcement by tier.
- Added a small e2e fixture to ensure rollout promotion is blocked when policy requires full gates.

Example (sanitized)
- Policy summary string shown in API responses:
  "tier=enterprise -> require_full_gate_for=[batching,cache,quant_profile], require_smoke_gate_for=[batching,cache,quant_profile], overrides=admin_or_enterprise_approver"

Rollout plan
- Ship policy plumbing + defaults behind existing gating feature flag.
- For limited production rollout, only apply tier defaults for newly created routes; existing routes remain pinned via backfill bindings.

Follow-ups
- Add account_id bindings once we have a stable enterprise exceptions workflow (see PM-482).
- Add policy observability counters (policy_resolution_source, required_tier) once ENG-3926 lands.

Refs
- Related PRs: pr-19531 (block apply when gate fails), pr-19544 (rollout controller respects gate), pr-19537 (override flow)
- Linear: ENG-3921, ENG-3928; PM: PM-481

title: Prompt for + pin related SUP tickets and customer-impact summary in incident bridge channels
preview: Context: Support/SRE asked for a consistent way to keep customer escalations discoverable after a bridge ends. Today, bridge channels frequently miss links to the SUP ticket(s), and the customer-impact summary gets repeated across Slack/Jira/email with drift. This PR updates incident-bot to (1) prompt the bridge creator for related SUP tickets and a customer-impact summary, (2) post and pin a single "Escalation context" message in the bridge channel, and (3) keep the channel topic synced with SUP links (without including customer names). Behavior: - When a bridge channel is created via the bot (or when the bot detects it has been invited to a newly created bridge channel), the bot posts a message with two required actions: "Add related Jira tickets" (accepts SUP-* and optionally INT-*), and "Add customer impact" (free text w/ guidance). - Once submitted, the bot edits the message into a structured summary and pins it. - The bot also sets the channel topic to include: "SUP: SUP-xxxx, SUP-yyyy" plus a short impact tag (e.g., "impact: elevated 5xx, eu-west") and a link to the escalation playbook section on bridges. - The message includes a reminder not to paste customer-sensitive details; it focuses on symptoms/impact/request IDs. - The message provides a one-click "Update context" action to edit tickets/impact later; updates preserve history in a small "edited" footer line with timestamp + editor. Guardrails: - Only allows keys matching ^SUP-\d+$ (and ^INT-\d+$) unless the user explicitly selects "Add other Jira key" (disabled by default). - If no SUP tickets are provided within 10 minutes, the bot posts a follow-up nudge tagging the bridge creator and the current on-call group (configurable per workspace env). - If channel is moved/renamed into #incidents conventions, the pinned message remains and is re-pinned if it was unpinned. Notes: This is intentionally not doing Jira API lookups (permissions vary and we want to avoid leaking summaries into Jira unintentionally); it’s just linking + standardizing context in Slack. Follow-ups: INT-333 will enforce required fields on the Jira side; this PR is the Slack-side piece. Testing: added unit tests for parsing keys, pin idempotency, and topic formatting; added an integration test stub for Slack modal submissions (mocked Slack client).

title: Add representative workload suite to nightly canary + gating mode for release branches
preview: Context: As part of the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project, we need the representative suite to run continuously (nightly) and also to be usable as a release gate for runtime release branches. This PR wires the representative suite runner into perf-canary with two execution modes: (1) nightly mode (signal + trend) and (2) gating mode (pass/fail) intended for release/* branches. Summary of changes: - Adds a new job definition for the representative suite (chat streaming, long-context prefill, embeddings high-QPS; tool-calling is stubbed in config but not enabled by default yet). - Introduces a `mode` flag: `nightly` vs `gating`. Nightly publishes full metric payloads and trends; gating evaluates a smaller set of canonical metrics against threshold config and emits a single PASS/FAIL with links to artifacts. - Adds threshold configuration files keyed by (model_family, workload, gpu_sku, region) with explicit metric boundaries: TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95, throughput (tokens/s or req/s for embeddings), plus guardrail saturation signals. - Adds result reporting integration: JSON summary + a markdown report posted to the run output (and to the existing perf-canary comment bot when invoked from a PR workflow). - Adds a “compare-to-baseline” helper that reads the baseline anchor from the run manifest (per ADR-0172) and computes deltas with a simple noise model (min sample size + absolute and relative thresholds). - Adds routing behavior: nightly runs post to #perf-canary (non-paging); gating runs post to #perf-triage (non-paging) and only page on repeated failures (3 consecutive gating failures on the same release branch + same cohort). Important operational notes: - Gating is intentionally fail-closed for release branches (i.e., it will block promotion) but has an explicit override mechanism via a signed config exception (see `docs/gating-exceptions.md`). - We treat missing baseline for a cohort as “SKIP” (non-blocking) to avoid halting releases when a new cohort is added before the baseline sweep catches up. - Cold vs warm cache: gating evaluates both when available; nightly always reports both. By default, gating fails only on cold regressions (warm regressions are warnings) until we finish calibrating cache variance across SKUs. Related: - Linear: ENG-4828 - Confluence: Perf regression policy & thresholds (company standard) - ADR-0172 run manifest schema (consumed by this runner) Testing: - Added unit tests for threshold parsing + delta evaluation. - Ran a dry-run on us-east/H100 with a known-good baseline anchor; verified PASS in gating mode and successful artifact upload. - Simulated a regression by pinning an older baseline + forcing a degraded threshold; verified FAIL output and Slack routing. Rollout plan: - Enable nightly representative suite runs first (starting next scheduled window) and observe variance panels for 1 week. - Enable gating mode for `release/runtime-*` branches after Sana/Sean confirm calibrated thresholds for H100 + A100 cohorts. - Expand to eu-west once baseline sweep completes for that region. Notes / TODO: - Tool/function-calling workload is present in config but not scheduled; will land separately once the generator stabilizes. - Some cohorts are commented out pending L40S baseline completion.

title: Improve streaming disconnect errors (request ID propagation + clearer classification)
preview: Context: Support escalations around streaming resets/truncation (notably us-east) have been slowed by ambiguous client-visible errors and missing request IDs in some disconnect paths. This PR improves server-side error classification for streaming disconnects and ensures request ID propagation is consistent across gateway -> runtime -> client responses/logs. The goal is to let Support quickly distinguish (a) client/proxy disconnects, (b) upstream runtime cancellations, and (c) internal gateway timeouts, and to reliably hand customers a request_id they can paste back into tickets. Changes: (1) Introduce a dedicated StreamingDisconnect error category with stable public error codes and safe-to-share messaging. (2) Guarantee request_id inclusion in error payloads for streaming responses (including mid-stream termination) and in trailing metadata where applicable. (3) Improve logging: emit a single structured log line on stream termination with reason enum + timing + bytes/tokens sent (no customer payload). (4) Normalize mapping between internal cancellation reasons and public-facing error codes to reduce misclassification as 500. Notes: No customer-specific details in messages; wording reviewed with Support/CS expectations. Follow-ups: docs/SDK troubleshooting updates are tracked separately (see redwood-docs PRs).

title: perf-canary: add paging (Sev2/Sev1) alert rules for sustained per-model regressions
preview: Summary: This adds paging-tier perf-canary alert rules (Sev2 + Sev1) for sustained regressions on top models. These are intentionally stricter than the warning rules in pr-19179 (higher minimum sample size, longer sustain window, and stricter anti-flap) and route to the on-call rotation channel via incident-bot. Motivation: During phase-1 rollout we saw warning-level regressions that were actionable when they sustained (real tail-latency impact), but the existing rules were designed to be noisy-tolerant and non-paging. This PR implements the paging path that SRE approved in the paging policy thread and is required for rollout gates integration. Alerting model: We page only for top-model + sustained regressions where we have enough canary volume to reduce false positives, and we gate on a canary-health signal (telemetry freshness + request loop healthy) to avoid paging on broken canary. Key behaviors: 1) Severity levels: Sev2 (page) for sustained p95 latency regression OR sustained tokens/sec drop on top models; Sev1 (page) only if regression is severe and sustained across consecutive windows. 2) Attribution-friendly annotations: every alert includes model, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_tag (if present), region, hardware_sku, baseline id, observed vs baseline deltas, and deep links to the Grafana dashboard + runbook. 3) Anti-flap + dedupe: minimum sample size, sustain windows, and incident-bot dedupe keys based on (model, region, hardware_sku, signal, severity). 4) Routing: Sev2/Sev1 route to #eng-oncall and create an incident-bot thread tagged perf-canary; warning-level continues to #eng-runtime. Thresholds (initial defaults, tuned for us-east phase-1): p95_e2e_latency_ms regression: Sev2 if >= +15% over baseline for 20m AND samples >= 200 AND canary_health=ok; Sev1 if >= +30% for 30m AND samples >= 300 AND canary_health=ok. tokens_per_sec regression: Sev2 if <= -12% for 25m AND samples >= 150; Sev1 if <= -20% for 35m AND samples >= 250. title: Add Shadow Traffic quality delta dashboards + scoring lag panels (cardinality-safe)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a first-cut set of Grafana dashboards to visualize Shadow Traffic quality deltas (control vs shadow variants) and scoring pipeline lag/health. The dashboards are designed for Hosted/Dedicated/Private and intentionally avoid high-cardinality breakdowns (no prompt_id, request_fingerprint, raw route_id, customer_id).

This is intended to support beta oncall readiness and help triage the two main failure classes seen during dogfood/beta:
1) quality deltas missing/stale due to scoring lag or ingestion lag
2) metrics backend pressure due to accidental high-cardinality dimensions

## What’s included
- New "Shadow Traffic / Quality Deltas" dashboard:
  - Δ quality score (shadow - control) over time with confidence bands (when emitted)
  - refusal rate delta, format adherence delta
  - sample size panels (n_control, n_shadow) + effective sample rate
  - breakdowns limited to:
    - deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private)
    - model_family (e.g., llama3, qwen2, mistral)
    - route_group (sanitized bucket: "core_chat", "embeddings", "tools", etc.)
    - rubric_version (pinned)
    - experiment_id (capped / top-k only)

- New "Shadow Traffic / Scoring Lag & Worker Health" dashboard:
  - scoring queue lag (p50/p95/p99) + oldest message age
  - worker throughput, retry rate, DLQ count
  - ingestion lag for shadow_pair events
  - circuit breaker open rate + dropped shadow attempts (fail-open)

- Alert rule proposals (NOT enabled by default):
  - scoring pipeline lag SLO breach (ticket -> page escalation guidance in annotations)
  - missing scoring signal (no scored pairs for X minutes while shadow attempts > 0)
  - quality regression advisory alert (requires minimum sample size + CI)

## Cardinality / safety notes
This PR follows the cardinality constraints discussed in SUP-28510 and the Shadow Metrics ADR:
- No dashboards query by prompt_id/request_fingerprint.
- experiment_id panels use a top-k query and default to aggregate view.
- route_id is replaced with route_group generated upstream (low-cardinality mapping maintained by telemetry).
- model_id is avoided; we use model_family + (optional) variant_role (control|shadow).

## Files changed (high level)
- dashboards/shadow_traffic_quality_deltas.jsonnet
- dashboards/shadow_scoring_lag_and_health.jsonnet
- dashboards/_library/shadow_traffic_panels.libsonnet
- alerts/shadow_scoring_lag_rules.yml (proposed)
- alerts/shadow_quality_regression_rules.yml (proposed)
- dashboards/README.md (usage + label guidance)

## Example panel queries (illustrative)
- Quality delta (aggregate):
  sum(rate(redwood_shadow_quality_score_sum{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_shadow_quality_score_count{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m]))
  -
  sum(rate(redwood_control_quality_score_sum{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_control_quality_score_count{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode", rubric_version=~"$rubric_version"}[5m]))

- Scoring lag (p95):
  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, deployment_mode) (rate(redwood_shadow_scoring_lag_seconds_bucket{deployment_mode=~"$deployment_mode"}[5m])))

## Rollout plan
- Merge to observability-pack, dashboards deployed to internal Grafana first (dogfood tenants)
- Enable for Dedicated beta tenants once telemetry labels are confirmed stable (route_group + model_family present)
- Alert rules remain "proposed" until SRE signs off on thresholds and paging policy

## Screenshots
Not included in PR description (dashboards are generated in CI and reviewed via preview JSON in artifacts). See CI artifact: grafana-dashboards-preview.zip

## Checklist
- [x] Panels avoid high-cardinality dimensions
- [x] Default views are aggregate-first; breakdowns are opt-in
- [x] Added dashboard annotations linking to runbook sections (shadow scoring lag / missing data)
- [x] CI renders jsonnet and validates Grafana schema

## Notes / follow-ups
- Follow-up: add a small "label sanitizer" doc with examples of safe/unsafe breakdowns (Logan/Nadia request)
- Follow-up: once confidence interval emission is standardized, switch CI panel to render CI bands instead of placeholder series

Related:
- ENG-5409 (alerting quality regression detection)
- ENG-5410 (cardinality guardrails for shadow metrics)
- SUP-28477 (missing quality dashboard data for Dedicated tenant)
- SUP-28510 (cardinality alerts after enabling shadow traffic)

title: Fix apply/rollback race condition causing mixed worker optimization states
preview:
Context: During the rollback gameday + a customer escalation (SUP-18503), we reproduced a race where an operator-triggered rollback could overlap with an in-flight apply stage. In certain interleavings, some workers would commit the new optimization config while others would revert to the previous version, leaving the fleet in a mixed state until the next periodic reconcile. This PR makes the runtime apply pipeline linearizable per (tenant, model, region) scope by introducing an apply-epoch token and CAS-style commit semantics at the worker checkpoint boundary.

What changes: (1) Control plane now includes a monotonic `apply_epoch` in the runtime apply intent payload; apply and rollback both bump epoch. (2) Workers persist the last committed (config_version, apply_epoch) and will only commit intents with epoch >= current; older intents are treated as no-ops and still ACKed for idempotent retries. (3) Apply pipeline checkpoint is updated so "download + validate" can run speculatively, but "commit" is gated on epoch comparison and a per-scope mutex to avoid mixed state inside a worker when concurrent reconciles fire. (4) Rollback now cancels any in-flight apply for the same scope by bumping epoch and emitting a `rollback_supersedes_apply` marker used by the rollout controller.

Why this fixes the bug: previously the pipeline compared only config_version and assumed apply/rollback were mutually exclusive at the orchestrator. Under retries, controller restarts, or delayed worker polls, a stale apply intent could arrive after rollback and overwrite the rollback state on a subset of workers. With epoch monotonicity + CAS commit, stale intents cannot regress the committed state.

Tests: adds deterministic concurrency tests that simulate (a) apply and rollback crossing in time with delayed worker polls, (b) controller retry storms, and (c) partial apply followed by rollback while some workers are mid-download. The new tests fail on main and pass with this patch.

Operational notes: This is a backwards-compatible change with a safe default epoch=0 for older orchestrator payloads. After rollout, we should see fewer "mixed_version" warnings in runtime logs. This is a hotfix candidate for the Limited GA safe-apply flag cohort.

Follow-ups: a separate PR will plumb epoch through Console rollouts UI and add a metric dimension on `optcfg_apply_epoch` for debugging (not required for correctness).

title: Console: Shadow experiment results page (quality deltas + example drilldowns) behind feature flag
preview:
## Summary
Adds the Console UI for viewing Shadow Experiment results: quality deltas vs control, confidence intervals, segment breakdowns, and a drilldown drawer for scored examples.

This is explicitly *read-only* UI for the beta. Creation flow remains in the existing experiment wizard (DES-611 work), and the results page is linked from Rollouts timeline + the Shadow Experiments list.

Everything in this PR is gated behind `shadow_experiments_results_ui` (per tenant). When disabled, routes fall back to the existing placeholder/"Coming soon" page.

## Why
We have shadow traffic shipping in dogfood and early Dedicated beta, but teams can’t validate quality changes without an at-a-glance view of deltas and a way to inspect examples. This PR wires up the results UX to the new aggregates + examples endpoints from the Console backend (PR #24933) and renders:
- Quality delta charts (candidate vs control), with 95% CI bands and significance callout when applicable
- Segment filters (safe dimensions only) + time range picker
- "Top regressions" table and per-metric deep links
- Example drilldown drawer (prompt/response *redacted*) with rubric breakdowns and scorer metadata

## Feature flag
- Flag: `shadow_experiments_results_ui`
- Default: off
- Enabled for: internal dogfood tenant only (handled via INT-912 / config rollout)

## UX highlights
- Results header: Experiment name, route, control target, shadow target(s), sampling %, rubric version, data retention indicator
- Metric cards: e.g. `format_adherence_rate`, `refusal_rate`, `instruction_following_score_mean`, `safety_policy_violation_rate`
- Chart behavior:
  - Shows control and candidate series + delta band view toggle
  - Confidence band computed server-side; UI simply renders (no client stats)
  - Tooltips show sample size (n), window, and whether samples are shadow-sampled vs prompt-set
- Segment filters (cardinality-safe): `region`, `request_type`, `model_variant`, `status_class` (2xx/4xx/5xx), `token_bucket` (bucketed)
  - Explicitly *not* supported in UI: `prompt_id`, `customer_id`, raw `route_id` free text (route is chosen from context)

## API wiring
- GET `/console/v1/shadow-experiments/:id/aggregates?window=1h&range=24h&segment=region`
- GET `/console/v1/shadow-experiments/:id/examples?metric=format_adherence_rate&outcome=regression&limit=50&cursor=...`
- Error/empty states:
  - If scoring lag or no data: show "Waiting for scoring" with link to troubleshooting docs (internal)
  - If RBAC denies `shadow:read_examples`: hide examples tab and show callout

## Implementation details
- Adds `ShadowExperimentResultsPage` route + navigation entry under Observability → Shadow Experiments
- New components:
  - `QualityDeltaChart` (line + band)
  - `MetricDeltaCard` (delta, CI, sample size)
  - `SegmentFilterBar` (safe enum dims only)
  - `ScoredExampleDrawer` (prompt/response + rubric breakdown)
- Uses existing design system primitives (Panels, Callouts, Tables, Skeleton loaders)
- Maintains strict PII posture:
  - UI never renders raw prompt/response unless backend returns redacted text (expected)
  - Displays explicit "Redacted" markers when fields omitted

## Tests
- Unit tests:
  - chart toggle behavior (series vs delta)
  - segment filter param mapping + resets
  - flag gating renders placeholder when off
- Playwright:
  - loads results page, applies time range + segment, opens example drawer

## Screenshots (internal)
- Results overview: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-overview
- Segment breakdown: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-segments
- Example drawer: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-example

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable for internal tenant only (ENG-5416)
3) Enable for 2 Dedicated beta customers after SRE signoff on scoring lag dashboards (ENG-5409)

## Checklist
- [x] Feature flag gating added
- [x] RBAC checked before showing example drilldowns
- [x] Empty/scoring-lag states
- [x] Segment dimensions constrained to allowlist
- [x] Basic Playwright coverage

## Related
- Backend APIs: PR #24933
- Dashboards: observability-pack PR #391
- ADRs: adr-shadow-metrics-data-model-and-cardinality, quality-metrics-observability-standard

---

### Commit summary
1. console: add shadow experiment results route + nav entry
2. console: implement quality delta chart + metric cards
3. console: add segment filter bar with dimension allowlist
4. console: add scored examples table + drawer (RBAC gated)
5. console: wire up API client + pagination + retries
6. console: add tests (unit + Playwright)

### Changed areas (high level)
- `console/apps/web/src/routes/shadowExperiments/ShadowExperimentResultsPage.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/components/quality/QualityDeltaChart.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/components/quality/ScoredExampleDrawer.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/api/shadowExperiments.ts`
- `console/apps/web/src/flags/featureFlags.ts`
- `console/apps/web/tests/playwright/shadowExperimentsResults.spec.ts`

Small illustrative snippet (flag gating):

if (!flags.shadow_experiments_results_ui) {
  return <ShadowExperimentsResultsPlaceholder />;
}

### CI
- web:lint ✅
- web:typecheck ✅
- web:test ✅
- web:playwright ✅ (reran once due to flake; see conversation)

### Review conversation (highlights)
Kenji Nakamura (requested changes): "Chart density is a bit high; can we default to 24h and keep 7d behind a secondary selector? Also the CI band needs a lighter tint to match the rest of the Console charts."

Dylan Brooks (author): "Updated defaults to 24h and moved 7d/30d into the time-range dropdown. Adjusted band opacity to 12% and used the `--chart-band` token. Pushed in 5f7c3b1."

Allison Grant: "Segment filter list looks safe but please hard-block arbitrary querystring dims; we’ve been burned by people copy/pasting links with `segment=prompt_id`."

Dylan Brooks: "Added allowlist validation + fallback to `none` with a toast. Also added a small unit test for invalid segment values."

Sean Gallagher: "Make sure example drawer doesn’t fetch examples automatically on page load; this can spike load when people share links."

Dylan Brooks: "Good call — examples now fetch only when the Examples tab is opened, and we cap initial load at 25 with explicit pagination."

Chloe Martin: "API client should pass `rubric_version` through for display, but don’t use it to branch UI logic yet."

Dylan Brooks: "Wired `rubric_version` into header display only. No conditional rendering."

Marissa Cole: "Can we add a callout making it clear this doesn’t affect user traffic and is asynchronous?" 

Dylan Brooks: "Added info callout under header: 'Shadow results are computed asynchronously and may lag by up to N minutes.' (N pulled from backend config)."

CI note: Playwright failed once with a timeout on opening the example drawer in staging; reran and passed. Added an extra wait on the skeleton -> content transition.

### Merge
Merged via squash into `main`.

title: Add Splunk HEC export target (batching, retry/backoff, delivery metrics)
preview:
## Summary
Adds a first-class Splunk HEC export target to audit-log-exporter. The target supports (1) bounded batching to control request volume and Splunk HEC payload size, (2) retry w/ exponential backoff + jitter for transient failures, and (3) explicit delivery visibility via metrics and structured logs.

This is part of the audit logging standardization effort to ensure customers can reliably export normalized audit events into common SIEMs. Splunk HEC is a frequent enterprise requirement (see SUP-18477) and has been a blocker for at least two Private-deployment evaluations.

## Motivation / context
- Customers want to ingest audit logs into Splunk with predictable field semantics and stable delivery.
- Our current exporter supports HTTP generic sinks, but Splunk HEC has specific behavior and recommended payload structure (event wrapping, source/sourcetype, token auth, and status codes that require careful retry handling).
- We also need exporter-side metrics to troubleshoot "events missing" vs "events delayed" vs "events duplicated" reports.

## Design notes
### Delivery semantics
- Exporter remains at-least-once for supported sinks. Splunk HEC requests are retried on transient errors and select 5xx/429 responses.
- We do NOT attempt exactly-once. Deduplication is supported via stable `event_id` and consistent batching behavior; customers should dedupe on `event_id` downstream (aligns with SUP-18510 guidance).

### Splunk HEC payload format
We use the standard HEC /services/collector endpoint with JSON events in the form:
- `event`: the original audit event JSON (already normalized upstream)
- `time`: derived from audit event `timestamp` (unix seconds, float)
- Optional metadata fields: `host`, `source`, `sourcetype`, `index`

We intentionally do not flatten the audit event or remap top-level keys here; normalization happens earlier (tracked in ENG-4827). This PR focuses on transport + operational correctness.

### Batching
- Configurable max events per batch and max bytes per batch.
- Flush triggers: size threshold, byte threshold, or max flush interval.
- Conservative defaults tuned for Splunk HEC typical limits (keeps payloads < ~900KB by default).

### Retry / backoff
- Exponential backoff with jitter.
- Retries on: network errors, 408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504.
- No retry on: 400/401/403/404 (treat as configuration/auth issue), unless explicitly forced by `retry_on_4xx` (default false).
- Backpressure: the exporter queue will grow up to `queue.max_size`. Once full, we drop with explicit counters and structured error logs (same pattern as other sinks).

### Failure visibility metrics
Added metrics (Prometheus) under the existing exporter namespace:
- `audit_exporter_sink_requests_total{sink="splunk_hec",status="success|error",code="..."}`
- `audit_exporter_sink_request_duration_seconds_bucket{sink="splunk_hec"}`
- `audit_exporter_sink_events_sent_total{sink="splunk_hec"}`
- `audit_exporter_sink_events_failed_total{sink="splunk_hec",reason="..."}`
- `audit_exporter_sink_batch_size_events{sink="splunk_hec"}` (histogram)
- `audit_exporter_queue_dropped_events_total{sink="splunk_hec"}`

Also added structured logs with stable fields: `sink`, `endpoint`, `hec_ack`, `attempt`, `backoff_ms`, `batch_events`, `batch_bytes`, `http_status`.

## Configuration
New sink type: `splunk_hec`

Example:
- `sinks:
    - type: splunk_hec
      name: splunk-prod
      endpoint: https://splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector
      token_env: SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN
      index: redwood_audit
      sourcetype: redwood:audit:v1
      source: redwood-audit-exporter
      host: ${HOSTNAME}
      batch:
        max_events: 500
        max_bytes: 900000
        flush_interval_ms: 1000
      retry:
        max_attempts: 8
        base_backoff_ms: 200
        max_backoff_ms: 10000
        retry_on_4xx: false
      tls:
        ca_bundle_path: /etc/ssl/certs/ca-bundle.crt
        insecure_skip_verify: false`

Notes:
- `token_env` is required; we do not allow a raw token literal in config to reduce accidental secrets exposure.
- Endpoint must be HTTPS by default; HTTP is allowed only if `allow_insecure_http=true` (intended for lab environments).

## Tests
- Unit tests:
  - payload wrapping and time parsing from RFC3339 timestamp
  - batching thresholds (events + bytes) and flush interval
  - retry classification matrix for status codes and network errors
  - token lookup and redaction in logs
- Integration-ish tests (mock server):
  - simulates 503->success with retry
  - simulates 401 and asserts no retry + surfaced config error
  - validates metrics emission and counters increment as expected

## Changed areas
- `src/sinks/splunk_hec.rs`: new sink implementation
- `src/config/sinks.rs`: config schema and validation
- `src/metrics.rs`: sink metrics additions
- `src/retry.rs`: shared helper updated to support per-sink override of retry-on-4xx
- `docs/sinks/splunk-hec.md`: setup + troubleshooting guide
- `examples/private/splunk-hec-values.yaml`: example Helm values (Private)

## Security considerations
- HEC token is treated as a secret: never logged; config printing and error messages redact it.
- We do not log raw event payloads on errors. Error logs include event counts and event_id sampling limited to 5 IDs per batch (behind debug level), to avoid accidental PII expansion.

## Rollout plan
- Phase 1: enable in staging for Hosted exporter and validate metrics + retry behavior under induced 503s.
- Phase 2: ship behind feature flag `exporter.sinks.splunk_hec.enabled` for Private charts.
- Phase 3: document customer guidance (PM-939) and share config snippets with Solutions Engineering.

## Reviewer notes / discussion highlights
- Allison requested we avoid high-cardinality labels. We removed `endpoint` as a metrics label (kept it only in logs).
- Sean asked for explicit dropped-event counters and a clear failure mode when queue is full; added `audit_exporter_queue_dropped_events_total` and a WARN summary every 60s during sustained drops.
- Hanae requested stricter TLS defaults; HTTP now requires `allow_insecure_http=true` and emits a startup WARN with a link to docs.

## Commits
1) config: add splunk_hec sink type and validation
2) sinks: implement Splunk HEC client + payload wrapper
3) retry: backoff + retry classification for HEC
4) metrics/logging: add sink counters, histograms, and structured logs
5) tests: unit + mock-server retries and auth failure behavior
6) docs/examples: Splunk setup, Private values example

## CI
- build: pass
- unit tests: pass
- lint/format: pass
- security scans (secrets + dependency audit): pass

Merged via squash as: "Add Splunk HEC export target (batching, retry/backoff, delivery metrics)"

title: Add schema_version tagging, JSON Schema validation, and legacy event normalization to v1
preview:
## Summary
This PR updates the audit-log-exporter to:
- Tag all outgoing events with `schema_version` (default: `v1.0`).
- Validate outgoing payloads against the canonical Audit Log Schema v1 (JSON Schema) and emit exporter-side metrics when validation fails.
- Normalize legacy/heterogeneous producer payloads into the v1 canonical shape to eliminate field drift across event types.

This is the first step to making SIEM exports consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments and to support SOC2/ISO evidence expectations (stable schema + documented backcompat behavior).

## Motivation
We have multiple event producers emitting “almost the same” semantics but with different field names and shapes. Customers exporting to Splunk/Sentinel/Datadog see inconsistent keys across event types (SUP-18477). Additionally, exporter retries can cause duplicates (SUP-18510); while idempotency is ultimately a downstream concern, consistent `event_id` + stable schema helps customers dedupe.

## What changed
1) schema_version tagging
- Add `schema_version` to every exported event.
- If an incoming event already includes `schema_version`, we preserve it (but still normalize/validate).
- If missing, exporter sets `schema_version` to `v1.0` after normalization.

2) JSON Schema validation (export-time)
- Added a `validator` module that loads the Audit Log Schema v1 JSON Schema artifact.
- Validation happens after normalization and before delivery to sinks.
- On validation failure:
  - Increment `audit_exporter_event_validation_failed_total{reason=...}`
  - Emit a structured log with `event_id`, `event_type`, `tenant_id`, and `validation_error_summary`
  - Continue delivery behavior is configurable:
    - Default: "best-effort" delivery (do not drop), but attach `exporter_warnings=["schema_validation_failed"]` for downstream visibility.
    - Optional: strict mode (drop invalid events) for internal testing environments.

3) Normalization (legacy -> v1 canonical)
- Introduced a canonical mapping layer that transforms known legacy fields into v1 fields:
  - `account_id` -> `tenant_id`
  - `org_id` -> `tenant_id` (only when `account_id` absent; logs warning if both present and disagree)
  - `user` / `user_email` / `actor_email` -> `actor.display_name` or `actor.email` (only if present; avoid synthesizing PII)
  - `ip` / `sourceIp` -> `source.ip`
  - `requestId` / `x_request_id` -> `request_id`
  - `traceId` -> `trace_id`
  - `type` / `action` -> `event_type` (mapped via allowlist registry)
  - `timestamp` (seconds) / `ts` -> `occurred_at` (RFC3339)
- Adds a deterministic `event_id` if missing (ULID) and preserves producer-provided `event_id` if present.
- Adds a stable `export_id` (UUID) per export attempt for debugging exporter retries.

4) Metrics + observability
- Added metrics:
  - `audit_exporter_events_normalized_total{schema_version=..., producer_hint=...}`
  - `audit_exporter_event_validation_failed_total{reason=...}`
  - `audit_exporter_event_schema_version_missing_total`
- Added debug-level logs for normalization decisions guarded by sampling (to avoid log spam).

## Example payload (after normalization)
Example shown here is illustrative; actual payload is flattened per existing exporter conventions, with a stable set of top-level keys. After normalization + tagging:
{"schema_version":"v1.0","event_id":"01J3N7KQKQ3QH7JQKJ0Z3V8M2M","event_type":"rbac.role.updated","occurred_at":"2026-02-04T18:22:11.219Z","tenant_id":"tnt_8f3c...","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"usr_2a91...","actor_email_hash":"sha256:...","source_ip":"203.0.113.10","request_id":"req_1f6d...","trace_id":"1-65b...","target_type":"role","target_id":"role_admin","action_result":"success"}

Notes:
- Email is not exported as raw PII; exporter hashes to `actor_email_hash` when configured and when input contains email. This aligns with ongoing redaction guidelines.
- `schema_version` is always present.

## Configuration changes
- `export.validation.mode`: `best_effort` (default) | `strict`
- `export.normalization.enabled`: true (default)
- `export.normalization.allow_legacy_event_types`: false (default). When true, legacy `type/action` strings can pass through if unmapped, but are tagged with `exporter_warnings=["unknown_event_type"]`.

## Testing
- Unit tests: normalization mapping cases (account/org id precedence, timestamp parsing, request/trace propagation, event_type mapping).
- Unit tests: JSON Schema validation pass/fail cases.
- Integration-ish tests: sink payload contains `schema_version` and stable required fields.

## Rollout plan
- Ship behind config toggles already supported by Helm values in Private.
- Start with Hosted exporter in best-effort mode, monitor validation failure metric.
- After one week with low failure rate, enable strict mode in CI and staging only.

## Risk/compat
- This PR is intentionally non-breaking for customers consuming exported events: it only adds `schema_version` and stabilizes field names.
- Some customers may have built dashboards keyed off legacy fields; normalization keeps the legacy fields out of the exported payload by default to reduce drift. If we see breakage, we can add a temporary `export.include_legacy_fields=true` compatibility switch (not included in this PR to avoid schema bloat).

## Links
- Linear: ENG-4827 (Exporter normalization + schema version tagging)
- Related: ENG-4833 (Legacy producer migration plan)
- Support: SUP-18477 (inconsistent fields), SUP-18510 (duplicates due to retries)

---
Checklist
- [x] Add schema_version tagging
- [x] Validate against schema v1 JSON Schema
- [x] Normalize known legacy producers
- [x] Add metrics + dashboards hooks
- [x] Document config toggles in exporter README

title: Retry-After parsing (delta-seconds + HTTP-date) + jittered exponential backoff
preview: Context: As part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative (retry/timeout standard), TS currently only parses Retry-After when it is a delta-seconds value. The gateway and some upstreams also emit Retry-After as an HTTP-date (e.g. "Wed, 21 Oct 2015 07:28:00 GMT"), which we should respect to avoid retrying too aggressively during throttling. In addition, our retry middleware used a deterministic exponential backoff sequence, which can create synchronized retries (thundering herd) across many clients. This PR adds: (1) Retry-After parsing for both delta-seconds and HTTP-date formats, (2) jittered exponential backoff consistent with the parity standard, and (3) tests/fixtures to lock behavior. Behavior details: Retry-After parsing: If header is delta-seconds => wait = clamp(deltaSeconds*1000, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If header is HTTP-date => wait = clamp(dateMs - nowMs, 0..maxRetryAfterMs). If parsing fails or results in NaN/negative => treat as absent and fall back to exponential backoff. We only honor Retry-After on status codes where we retry (429, 500/502/503/504), with primary focus on 429. Backoff + jitter: Introduce a jittered exponential backoff helper (baseDelayMs=250, multiplier=2, maxDelayMs=10_000) using full jitter: delay = randomInt(0..cap) where cap = min(maxDelayMs, baseDelayMs * 2^(attempt-1)). This matches the parity guidance and reduces retry synchronization under load. Backwards compatibility: No API surface changes; retry defaults remain the same maxAttempts. Only changes are (a) respecting additional valid Retry-After values and (b) adding jitter to the sleep, which may slightly change exact retry timing but improves reliability at scale. Testing: Adds unit tests for Retry-After parsing (seconds, HTTP-date, invalid, negative/0, far-future clamp) and for backoff jitter bounds. Also adds an integration-style test against the retry middleware with a simulated 429 + Retry-After HTTP-date to validate we wait at least the computed duration (with deterministic clock + stubbed RNG). Notes: This does not change streaming retry behavior (still conservative) and does not add any telemetry fields; those are tracked separately under ENG-5129.
