title: Shadow traffic: telemetry events + ingestion hooks (v1)
preview:
Context: Shadow traffic routing (ENG-5402) mirrors a sampled subset of prod requests to one or more shadow targets. To compute quality deltas (and to debug routing/scoring lag) we need a low-cardinality, privacy-aware set of telemetry events that (a) links the control and shadow inference outcomes, (b) can be ingested reliably in Hosted/Dedicated/Private, and (c) provides enough dimensions to slice by route/model/region without exploding metrics cardinality.

This PR introduces shadow traffic telemetry events and wires them into the ingestion pipeline (Kafka -> ingest -> warehouse tables) with initial dashboard stubs for internal dogfood.

What’s included: (1) New event schemas: shadow_pair_created (emitted when the mirror is scheduled), shadow_inference_completed (shadow target returned or timed out), and shadow_scoring_status (async scoring queued/completed/failed). Each event includes a stable shadow_pair_id and request_fingerprint (HMAC), plus required dimensions: tenant_id (hashed), route_id (low-card), control_model_id, shadow_target_id, deployment_mode, region, outcome, and timing fields. (2) Cardinality controls: disallow freeform tags in shadow telemetry, bucket user_agent, truncate prompt_length/response_length into fixed buckets, and explicitly drop prompt_id / conversation_id at emit-time. Route-level and model-level dimensions are included; anything higher-card must be attached via example sampling endpoints later (out of scope). (3) Ingestion pipeline hooks: adds validators/normalizers for the new events, forwards them through the existing ingest service, and creates the initial storage tables shadow_pairs_v1 and shadow_scoring_v1 (partitioned by day, clustered by tenant_hash + route_id). (4) Initial dashboards stubs: adds basic Grafana JSON stubs for shadow throughput, shadow target error rate, and scoring lag; these are intentionally minimal and will be expanded in observability-pack PR-391.

PII / data handling: This PR does not store raw prompts/responses. request_fingerprint is computed using an HMAC keyed per environment (see config), and tenant_id is hashed before leaving the data plane. Any headers included are allowlisted (content-type, accept, x-redwood-route) and explicitly exclude auth/cookie-like fields.

Testing: Unit tests for schema validation and normalization, golden tests for event-to-table mapping, and an end-to-end ingest test that emits a synthetic shadow_pair_created + shadow_inference_completed and asserts the warehouse row shape. Load testing / overhead benchmarking is tracked in ENG-5412.

Rollout plan: gated behind feature flag shadow_telemetry_v1 (default off). Dogfood tenants can enable via internal config. Fail-open: telemetry emission failures do not impact request path; ingestion lag only affects dashboards/metrics.

Follow-ups: (a) scoring payload references once redaction pipeline lands (ENG-5411), (b) additional aggregation views for confidence intervals, (c) tighten SLO alert thresholds once we have baseline dogfood data.

Notes for reviewers: please focus on (1) schema field choices and defaults, (2) cardinality guardrails, and (3) whether the ingestion mapping matches the query patterns we expect for Console drilldowns.

title: Perf baseline dashboards + alert rules for representative suite (TTFT/TPOT/throughput/variance) with manifest links
preview: Context: As part of the Q1'26 perf baseline + representative workload suite, we need a standard set of dashboards and alerting that (a) make baseline runs explorable, (b) make regressions actionable, and (c) are attributable via a first-class link from the panel to the run manifest + trace artifacts. This PR adds a versioned dashboard bundle and alert rules aimed at baseline runs + perf-canary outputs. What’s included: 1) Grafana dashboards (folder: "Perf Baselines") with panels for TTFT/TPOT percentiles, throughput, saturation proxies, and variance/noise views. 2) Alert rules for baseline regressions (non-paging by default) and canary gating signals (paging for release-branch gating only) with routing to the right channels/oncall. 3) Standard run label/templating conventions aligned with ADR-0172 (model, model_family, workload_id, region, sku, runtime_git_sha, scheduler_profile, kernel_profile, cache_mode, run_id). 4) Panel links: dashboard → run manifest (JSON) → trace bundle (if present). Links are built from run_id + artifact_base_url labels so we don’t have to hardcode per-environment paths. 5) A small metrics dictionary excerpt in-repo to keep dashboards stable while the full dictionary draft is iterated in Drive. Dashboards: - "Perf Baseline — Overview": top-line TTFT/TPOT p50/p95/p99 and throughput for the selected run cohort; quick filters for region/SKU/model/workload/cache_mode; includes a "cold vs warm" compare row. - "Perf Baseline — Latency detail": TTFT and TPOT with per-stage breakdown (client boundary vs server boundary where available), plus queue_wait_ms and batch_assembly_ms panels when the runtime emits them. - "Perf Baseline — Throughput & saturation": tokens/s, req/s (embeddings), GPU utilization (if present), batch size distribution, and a "saturation heuristics" row (queue depth, shed counters, backpressure). - "Perf Baseline — Variance": run-to-run variability panels (median absolute deviation + coefficient of variation) and a "noise budget" view to help separate regressions from fleet variability; includes warmup window visualization based on run_phase label. - "Perf Baseline — Regression compare": diff view against a selected baseline anchor (baseline_run_id) with percent deltas for p95/p99 TTFT/TPOT and throughput; flags when deltas exceed configured thresholds. Alerts: - perf_baseline_regression_warning: triggers when p99 TTFT or p99 TPOT regresses > 5% vs baseline anchor for a given (model, workload, region, sku, cache_mode) cohort, sustained over N=5 samples (configurable). Routes to #eng-runtime-perf-triage and tags the cohort in the alert annotations. - perf_baseline_variance_spike: triggers when variance (CV) exceeds threshold, indicating fleet instability; routes to #eng-sre-benchmark-fleet (non-paging). - perf_canary_gating_failure: only enabled for release branches (via label enable_gating=true); pages runtime oncall if p99 TTFT/TPOT regresses beyond gating thresholds for required cohorts. Notes on measurement boundaries: Dashboards assume TTFT/TPOT are server-side timings emitted by the benchmark harness/runtime instrumentation (not client wall-clock). We still show "first-token-on-wire" when present, but it’s not used for gating alerts. Manifest/trace linkouts: Panel links use a templated URL: ${artifact_base_url}/runs/${run_id}/manifest.json and ${artifact_base_url}/runs/${run_id}/traces/ (the trace link is conditional; shows as "Trace artifacts" with a warning when the label trace_available=false). Rollout plan: Merge behind folder-level RBAC (Engineering only). Alerts ship in disabled-by-default state for 48h dry run in staging; then enable warning alerts in prod; gating/paging remains controlled via enable_gating label and release branch policy. Testing: Validated dashboard JSON imports cleanly on grafana-staging and prod sandbox. Verified alert expressions against 2 historical baseline runs (chat_streaming and long_prefill) and confirmed annotation links resolve to manifest artifacts from benchmark-lab uploads. Screenshots: Attached in PR comments (overview + variance panel examples).

title: runtime/scheduler: enforce KV compaction budget at decode boundaries to avoid stalls
preview:
Context: This PR integrates KV-cache compaction budgeting into the scheduler loop to prevent decode-time stalls and tail-latency spikes when compaction/relocation work is triggered under load. It is part of the KV cache compaction improvements epic and builds on the allocator primitives + metrics added in earlier PRs (notably pr-19357 and pr-19342).

Problem: Prior compaction prototype ran relocation work opportunistically when fragmentation crossed a threshold. Under mixed-length workloads, the relocation work could happen during decode hot-path and cause intermittent stalls (visible as p99 spikes and occasional perf-canary failures). We need explicit guardrails so compaction work is budgeted and scheduled only in safe windows.

What changed:
- Introduced scheduler-level compaction budget enforcement with two budgets: time (microseconds) and bytes-moved per scheduling tick.
- Enforced compaction only at safe boundaries by default: after prefill completion and at decode step boundaries (i.e., between token steps, not mid-kernel).
- Added a compaction "work token" mechanism: scheduler hands the allocator/compactor an explicit budget each tick; compactor must stop when exhausted and return progress + remaining backlog.
- Added backpressure integration hooks: if the scheduler is in overload mode (high queue depth / p99 guard), compaction budget is reduced to 0 and we rely on eviction instead (handled in follow-up PR for tier-aware eviction).
- Added tracing spans around "scheduler_budget_grant" and "compaction_step" to correlate granted budgets with latency spikes (spans are gated by existing runtime tracing sampling).
- Added config knobs (runtime flags, not customer UI):
  - kv_compaction.enabled (bool)
  - kv_compaction.budget_us_per_tick (default: 200us hosted, 500us dedicated; overridable)
  - kv_compaction.budget_bytes_per_tick (default: 4MiB hosted, 8MiB dedicated; overridable)
  - kv_compaction.safe_windows (enum: prefill_only | decode_boundaries | always; default: decode_boundaries)
- Added a "hard stop": if a single compaction step would exceed remaining budget, compactor returns without doing partial relocation (avoids half-moved page bookkeeping complexity; allocator keeps candidates queued).

Performance / correctness:
- Correctness: compaction work is now driven by scheduler state transitions; we do not attempt to move pages while a sequence is actively decoding within the same tick. The compactor uses existing pinning rules and will skip pinned pages; it reports skipped counts.
- Benchmarks (A100-80GB, runtime_bench/kv_frag_mixed_len_burst, 20 runs):
  - Before (allocator-triggered opportunistic compaction): p99 decode latency +14.8% during burst phase; occasional >50ms stalls correlated with bytes_moved spikes.
  - After (budgeted scheduling): p99 decode latency +1.9% during burst phase; no >50ms stalls observed; throughput -0.4% (within noise).
  - Fragmentation reduction curve is slower but monotonic; the goal here is tail protection, not fastest defrag.
- CI: Added perf CI check that fails if p99 regression >5% on kv_frag_mixed_len_burst when kv_compaction.enabled=true (gated behind nightly perf lane; see checks below).

Operational notes:
- Feature remains behind kv_compaction.enabled and is off by default on main until canary.
- If oncall sees compaction-caused latency, recommended immediate mitigation is setting budget_us_per_tick=0 (keeps metrics/tracing but disables work), then flipping kv_compaction.enabled=false if needed.

Follow-ups:
- Tier-aware eviction integration and policy selection (ENG-4825).
- Dashboard panel updates for budget granted vs bytes moved correlation (ENG-4830).

Test plan:
- Unit: scheduler budget accounting, compactor stop conditions, safe-window gating.
- Integration: long-lived sessions + churn with cancellation; verified no mid-step relocation; verified no deadlocks.
- Perf: benchmark-lab kv_frag_mixed_len_burst and kv_frag_long_lived_chat; perf-canary scenario added in perf-canary repo (tracked separately).

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (1) correctness around safe windows and state transitions, (2) budget accounting edge cases when ticks are short, and (3) whether the defaults are conservative enough for Hosted while still making fragmentation progress.

title: runtime: tier-aware LRU eviction policy for KV cache (pinned sessions + tests)
preview:
Summary: Implements tier-aware LRU eviction for the serving runtime KV cache to make allocator behavior predictable under memory pressure and to reduce burst-driven tail latency caused by opportunistic/late evictions. The policy prefers evicting low-priority tiers first (e.g., free/trial) while preserving higher tiers (hosted paid / dedicated) and always respecting pinned sessions. This PR includes correctness-focused unit + integration tests and adds basic observability counters needed for rollout guardrails.

Motivation: During high churn with mixed sequence lengths, the KV arena frequently hits soft pressure and the existing eviction path behaves effectively FIFO-ish (depends on free-list ordering), which can evict recently-active sequences and trigger retries / cancellations at bad times. We also had a support report (SUP-28433) where pinned long-lived sessions were disproportionately impacted during bursts.

Design: 1) Tier-aware ordering: each active sequence is assigned an eviction tier based on request context (tier enum sourced from auth/customer routing). Eviction scans tiers from lowest to highest. Within a tier we evict LRU based on last_kv_access_step (monotonic scheduler step). 2) Pinned session guard: sequences marked pinned (explicit customer pin / long-lived session) are non-evictable unless we cross the hard OOM guardrail. In this PR we treat pinned as absolute non-evictable and let the allocator return PRESSURE_HARD -> scheduler backpressure (the follow-up compaction work will reduce how often we reach this). 3) Safety constraints: we never evict a sequence that is currently in an in-flight decode microbatch or mid-relocation; eviction operates only on sequences in a stable state. If a candidate becomes ineligible (state changes) we rescan with a bounded attempt budget.

Implementation notes: - Added EvictionIndex that maintains per-tier min-heaps keyed by last_access_step (LRU). Updates happen on decode step boundaries and on request state transitions (prefill->decode, end-of-sequence, cancellation). - Added configurable knobs (behind feature flag) in runtime_flags.yaml: kv_eviction_enabled, kv_eviction_scan_budget, kv_eviction_soft_pressure_bytes, kv_eviction_tier_weights (initially simple tier ordering; weights reserved for follow-up). - Integrated into kv_cache_arena pressure handling: on SOFT_PRESSURE we attempt eviction up to scan_budget candidates (bounded). If eviction frees enough pages, allocations proceed; otherwise we bubble PRESSURE_SOFT to scheduler for backpressure / admission control. - Observability: exported kv_evictions_total{tier,cause}, kv_eviction_scan_candidates_total, kv_eviction_pinned_skips_total, kv_eviction_ineligible_skips_total. Cause is one of: soft_pressure, hard_pressure, admin (future).

Tests: - Unit tests cover ordering (tier then LRU), monotonic step update behavior, pinned non-evictable behavior, and scan budget enforcement. - Integration test constructs a mixed-churn workload with a small arena and verifies: (a) pinned sequences remain alive, (b) low-tier sequences are preferentially evicted, (c) no token mismatch / stream corruption under repeated eviction, (d) allocator returns PRESSURE_SOFT (not crash/oom) when only pinned remain.

text:
Summary: Implements tier-aware LRU eviction for the serving runtime KV cache to make allocator behavior predictable under memory pressure and to reduce burst-driven tail latency caused by opportunistic/late evictions. The policy prefers evicting low-priority tiers first (e.g., free/trial) while preserving higher tiers (hosted paid / dedicated) and always respecting pinned sessions. This PR includes correctness-focused unit + integration tests and adds basic observability counters needed for rollout guardrails.

Motivation: During high churn with mixed sequence lengths, the KV arena frequently hits soft pressure and the existing eviction path behaves effectively FIFO-ish (depends on free-list ordering), which can evict recently-active sequences and trigger retries / cancellations at bad times. We also had a support report (SUP-28433) where pinned long-lived sessions were disproportionately impacted during bursts.

Design: 1) Tier-aware ordering: each active sequence is assigned an eviction tier based on request context (tier enum sourced from auth/customer routing). Eviction scans tiers from lowest to highest. Within a tier we evict LRU based on last_kv_access_step (monotonic scheduler step). 2) Pinned session guard: sequences marked pinned (explicit customer pin / long-lived session) are non-evictable unless we cross the hard OOM guardrail. In this PR we treat pinned as absolute non-evictable and let the allocator return PRESSURE_HARD -> scheduler backpressure (the follow-up compaction work will reduce how often we reach this). 3) Safety constraints: we never evict a sequence that is currently in an in-flight decode microbatch or mid-relocation; eviction operates only on sequences in a stable state. If a candidate becomes ineligible (state changes) we rescan with a bounded attempt budget.

Implementation notes: - Added EvictionIndex that maintains per-tier min-heaps keyed by last_access_step (LRU). Updates happen on decode step boundaries and on request state transitions (prefill->decode, end-of-sequence, cancellation). - Added configurable knobs (behind feature flag) in runtime_flags.yaml: kv_eviction_enabled, kv_eviction_scan_budget, kv_eviction_soft_pressure_bytes, kv_eviction_tier_weights (initially simple tier ordering; weights reserved for follow-up). - Integrated into kv_cache_arena pressure handling: on SOFT_PRESSURE we attempt eviction up to scan_budget candidates (bounded). If eviction frees enough pages, allocations proceed; otherwise we bubble PRESSURE_SOFT to scheduler for backpressure / admission control. - Observability: exported kv_evictions_total{tier,cause}, kv_eviction_scan_candidates_total, kv_eviction_pinned_skips_total, kv_eviction_ineligible_skips_total. title: Add mixed-length burst scenario + thresholds for KV compaction regressions
preview:
Motivation: The KV cache compaction work (runtime-1.22) needs a perf-canary signal that reliably reproduces fragmentation + churn patterns we see in production, and that fails fast when compaction/eviction introduces decode stalls or tail latency spikes. Existing canary scenarios (steady decode, long prompts) don’t create enough allocator churn to surface the regressions we saw in staging.

This PR adds a new scenario (`mixed_length_burst`) that generates mixed prompt lengths + bursty arrivals + cancellations, and wires pass/fail thresholds + alert routing that are specific to compaction regressions.

What’s included: - New canary scenario: mixed-length burst (configurable length distribution + burst profile) - KPI collection updates: capture p95/p99 latency, TTFT, throughput, and the runtime-exposed compaction/eviction signals when present (bytes moved / compaction time / eviction rate) - Thresholds + gating: scenario-specific failure criteria (tail latency + compaction correlation guardrails) - Alert routing: send failures to `#eng-sre` + `#eng-runtime` with distinct annotation so oncall can quickly identify compaction-related failures vs baseline infra noise - Docs: scenario description + how to interpret failures + links to dashboards

Notes/constraints: - This PR does not require compaction to be enabled; it will still run against baseline runtime versions. When compaction metrics are absent, the scenario gates on latency/throughput only and emits a warning annotation rather than failing. - Thresholds were calibrated using recent benchmark-lab results and staging canary runs; see “Calibration” below.

Calibration summary: - Target workload: mixed prefill lengths (p10~64 tok, p50~256 tok, p90~1.5k tok), decode lengths with a long tail, 20–40% burst uplift for 90s windows, 5–10% cancellation rate. - Failure criteria (initial): (a) p99 decode latency regression > 12% vs control build OR absolute p99 exceeds scenario SLO cap; (b) compaction_bytes_moved_per_s sustained > budget + correlated p99 spike; (c) eviction_rate sustained above threshold AND output token rate drops below floor.

Rollout plan: - Land scenario behind `--enable-scenario=mixed_length_burst` flag for one week - Enable in nightly canary for Hosted GPU pools first; then expand to Dedicated canary cohorts once we have stable baselines

Related: - ENG-4828 (scenario) - runtime PRs: compaction metrics/tracing are landing separately in the monorepo; this PR only consumes exported telemetry when available.

title: Runtime: KV compaction kill-switch + config plumbing (oncall-safe disable path)
preview:
Context: KV cache compaction/eviction is rolling out behind flags. During canaries we need a deterministic, oncall-friendly way to disable compaction immediately (or gate it to a safe minimum) without requiring a full runtime restart, while preserving allocator invariants and avoiding relocation-in-progress hazards.

This PR adds the config/feature-flag plumbing and a safe-disable path (“kill switch”) for KV compaction + related relocation behavior. It also wires metrics/tracing so oncall can validate that the disable took effect, and links to the runtime runbook + rollout guardrails.

What changed: (1) Introduced runtime config keys under `kv.compaction.*` with sane defaults for release/runtime-1.22: `kv.compaction.enabled` (bool), `kv.compaction.kill_switch` (bool, higher priority than enabled), `kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick` (int), `kv.compaction.budget_bytes_per_tick` (int), `kv.compaction.min_fragmentation_ratio` (float), and `kv.compaction.force_background_worker_off` (bool). (2) Added control-plane config subscription wiring to the runtime config registry so these keys can be flipped via existing rollout tooling; includes “last applied config version” and “last applied timestamp” tracking. (3) Implemented safe-disable semantics: kill-switch causes (a) scheduler to stop granting compaction budget immediately, (b) background worker to stop accepting new relocation tasks, and (c) relocation state machine to transition to a quiescent point (finish current page move if already in a copy/commit section, then stop). The goal is to avoid leaving partially-relocated blocks or dangling references; correctness is preferred over instantly aborting mid-copy. (4) Added oncall-exposed counters + gauges: `kv_compaction_config_kill_switch{}` (0/1), `kv_compaction_config_enabled{}` (0/1), `kv_compaction_config_version` (gauge), `kv_compaction_disable_inflight_moves_total` (counter), and `kv_compaction_disable_wait_ms` (histogram) so we can see whether we were blocked waiting to reach a safe point. (5) Tracing: added a lightweight span event `kv_compaction.kill_switch_applied` with config version + node id to correlate with any tail-latency spikes around a disable. (6) Docs pointers: updated internal docs links in code comments to `sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-kv-cache-compaction-runbook.json` and `hosted-api-tail-latency-guardrails.json` (SRE guardrails). No user-facing docs.

Operational behavior (intended): - If `kill_switch=true`, compaction does not run regardless of `enabled`. - If `enabled=false` (kill_switch false), compaction is disabled but we still export config metrics; relocation state machine remains loaded but idle. - If config propagation is delayed, nodes continue with previous config; metrics include applied version so rollout system can detect stragglers. - On shutdown/restart, persisted config is re-read at boot, so kill-switch is sticky across restarts.

Notes on safety: Kill-switch does not hard-cancel a relocation that is already in a critical section. Instead we wait for a “commit boundary” (copy -> pointer swap -> free old) and then stop. This avoids a correctness class where we’d lose KV pages or leave allocator free-list inconsistent. Oncall expectation: disable should apply within <100ms under normal load; we record if it takes longer.

Files/areas touched (high level): - `runtime/config/kv_compaction_config.{h,cc}` new config keys + validation - `runtime/control_plane/config_subscriber.cc` registry wiring + version tracking - `runtime/kv/compaction/compaction_worker.cc` kill-switch checks + quiesce - `runtime/kv/compaction/relocation_state_machine.cc` quiescent boundary handling - `runtime/scheduler/scheduler_loop.cc` compaction budget gating on kill-switch - `runtime/observability/metrics_kv_compaction.cc` config + disable metrics - `runtime/tracing/kv_compaction_spans.cc` event emission - tests: `runtime/kv/compaction/test_kill_switch.cc`, `runtime/config/test_kv_config_validation.cc`

Small illustrative snippet: `if (cfg.kv.compaction.kill_switch) { budget = 0; worker->RequestQuiesce("kill_switch"); }`

Testing: - Unit: config validation, kill-switch override ordering, quiesce boundary behavior - Integration: scheduler loop doesn’t grant budget with kill-switch on, even with fragmentation trigger - Soak: repeated flip enabled/disabled/kill_switch under synthetic churn; asserts no allocator invariant violations - Manual: verified config flip via rollout tool updates metrics within propagation window

Rollout plan: This is a prerequisite for canarying compaction allocator changes (PR-19357/19371). Landed in release/runtime-1.22 so we can use it during canary expansions. Kill-switch authority remains SRE oncall (per guardrails doc), with runtime TL as escalation.

Links: - Linear: ENG-4826 (kill-switch/config), ENG-4829 (release plan) - Jira: INT-11904 (dashboard standards follow-up), SUP-28433 (OOM/high churn mitigation path) - Related PRs: pr-19357 (allocator changes), pr-19371 (scheduler budgeting), pr-19405 (tracing spans)

Review conversation (highlights): - Sean Gallagher: asked for explicit metrics proving kill-switch applied + a bound on “time to quiesce.” Added `disable_wait_ms` histogram and documented expected behavior in comments. - Rafael Mendes: requested oncall steps + clarity on whether kill-switch persists across restart. Updated config boot path and added a note in runtime runbook link comment; verified sticky behavior. - Omar El-Sayed: questioned whether we should abort mid-move to reduce tail latency during incident. We chose correctness-first and added the counter `disable_inflight_moves_total` so we can see when we had to wait. - Allison Grant: asked to align metric naming with standards; renamed gauges to `kv_compaction_config_*` and added labels consistent with other runtime configs.

CI: First run failed `cfg-lint` due to missing docs link in new config file; fixed and reran. Final checks green: build (linux), unit tests, integration tests, sanitizer, config-lint.

Merge: Squash-merged into release/runtime-1.22. Backport not required (feature is new).

title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels
preview:
Title: Add /rollback shortcut command to post rollback UI + runbook links in incident channels

Context
During recent gamedays + the SUP-18421 escalation, the biggest time sink was not executing rollback itself, but getting responders to the right place quickly (Console rollback view + the canonical runbook) while context switching across tabs. This PR adds a tiny “fast path” in incident-bot: a slash command that posts the correct links and a short checklist directly into the incident channel.

What’s included
- New Slack slash command: `/rollback` (alias `/rw-rollback`).
- Posts a message with:
  - Link to Console Rollback UI (Optimization Config history view with the rollback modal entry point).
  - Link to the runbook: “Rollback Optimization Config”.
  - A reminder of the expected decision criteria (p95/p99 regression + 5xx) and the post-rollback verification checklist.
- Optional args:
  - `--env` (default: prod)
  - `--tenant <tenant_slug>`
  - `--model <model_id>`
  - `--region <region>`
  - `--scope <hosted|dedicated|private>` (default: hosted)
  These args don’t mutate anything; they’re used only to build a more specific Console deep link and to display the blast-radius header in the message.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - Only allowed in channels matching `^#incidents(-.*)?$` or `^#oncall(-.*)?$` (configurable via `INCIDENT_BOT_ALLOWED_CHANNELS_REGEX`).
  - If invoked elsewhere, command responds ephemerally with guidance and does not post any internal links.
  - If `--scope private` is provided, we do NOT link to hosted Console rollback UI; we instead post the private operator guide link and the generic runbook section.
  - Requests are logged (structured) with `request_id`, `slack_user_id`, `channel_id`, and parsed args to simplify debugging (no customer payloads).

Why incident-bot vs Console
This is intentionally not part of the rollback product surface area. It’s an operational shortcut for responders to reduce MTTR during active incidents while the Safe Apply + Rollback feature rolls out more broadly (PM-904).

Implementation notes
- Adds a new handler module `commands/rollback.ts` wired into the existing Slack command router.
- Centralizes link construction in `lib/links.ts` to avoid hardcoding Console hostnames across command handlers.
  - Uses `CONSOLE_BASE_URL` and `DOCS_BASE_URL` env vars.
  - Adds `consoleRollbackUrl({ env, tenant, model, region })` helper (returns a deep link with query params when provided).
- Adds a small args parser with strict allowlist flags (reject unknown flags and return help text).
- Includes unit tests for:
  - Channel allow/deny behavior
  - Arg parsing + help
  - Link construction for hosted/dedicated/private scopes

Screenshots (Slack output)
- Example: `/rollback --tenant northbridge --model llama-3.1-70b --region us-east-1`
  Posts:
  - “Rollback links (prod / hosted) — tenant=northbridge model=llama-3.1-70b region=us-east-1”
  - Button: “Open Rollback UI”
  - Button: “Rollback Runbook”
  - Bullet: “After rollback: verify p95 <= baseline + 5xx back to normal; annotate the incident timeline with config_version.”

Testing
- Unit: `pnpm test` (added coverage around new command router path).
- Lint/typecheck: `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck`.
- Manual:
  - Verified in #incidents-sandbox: message posts correctly and buttons render.
  - Verified in #random: command responds ephemerally and does not post links.

Rollout
- This is behind Slack app permission already granted to incident-bot; no new scopes required.
- Deploy with next incident-bot release (no migration).

Checklist
- [x] Unit tests added for allowlist and arg parsing
- [x] Verified command behavior in non-allowed channels (ephemeral response)
- [x] Links use env-configured base URLs (no hardcoding)
- [x] Reviewed with SRE for incident channel patterns

Commits
1) Add rollback slash command handler + routing
2) Add link helpers + env config
3) Add tests for command allowlist and link output

Changed areas (high level)
- src/commands/rollback.ts
- src/router/commands.ts
- src/lib/links.ts
- src/lib/args.ts
- test/rollback_command.test.ts
- config/env.example

Review conversation
- Sean Gallagher (requested changes): “Can we ensure we don’t leak internal Console URLs into customer shared channels? Some incident channels are bridged.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added channel regex allowlist + explicit deny behavior (ephemeral-only) and made it configurable via env.
- Hannah Rivera: “The copy should remind responders that rollback is a control-plane action and still needs confirmation in Console; avoid implying this command performs rollback.”
  - Aisha Bello: Updated message header + button labels to say ‘Open Rollback UI’ and added ‘This command does not execute rollback’ note.
- Marcus Lin: “Any authz concerns? At minimum, log invocations for auditability.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added structured log event `incident_bot.command.rollback.invoked` with request_id + slack identifiers. No tokens or sensitive params.

CI
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- lint: pass
- typecheck: pass

Merge notes
Squash merged after approvals. No runtime behavior changes outside Slack command surface. Safe to deploy independently of the control-plane rollback feature flag (PM-904), since this only links to UI/docs.

title: perf-canary: add QPS rate limits + per-region capacity budget (GPU overhead guardrail)
preview:
Context: As we move perf-canary from staging into a production region, we need hard guardrails so the canary cannot consume meaningful GPU capacity or accidentally fan out due to config mistakes. This PR implements (1) explicit QPS caps with a token-bucket limiter, and (2) a per-region capacity budget that converts an overhead target (e.g., 0.5% of region GPU capacity) into an allowed request rate/concurrency for the canary. The goal is: safe by default, predictable overhead, and an immediate kill-switch when budgets are exceeded.

What this adds (high-level): - Rate limiting: token bucket limiter at the request scheduler layer (covers both steady-state QPS and burst). - Capacity budget: `capacity_budget` config per region (defaults to 0.003 = 0.3% until we tune) with optional overrides per tier/model group. - Budget accounting: estimates GPU-seconds/request using (a) last known baseline runtime-only latency for the model+SKU, falling back to (b) a conservative static estimate when baseline is missing. - Concurrency guardrail: hard cap on in-flight requests derived from budget; limiter blocks and emits a "budget exceeded" metric instead of pushing load. - Safety valves: (a) global max_qps, (b) global max_in_flight, (c) "safe_disable" config flag to stop generating traffic while still emitting health metrics. - Telemetry: new counters/histograms so SRE can confirm overhead stays under budget and catch misconfig quickly.

Operational intent: The canary should stay <0.5% of GPU capacity per region for covered models (goal in project doc). We are starting with tighter defaults (0.3% budget, max 0.6 rps per model group, max 4 in-flight) and will loosen only after we see stable overhead in us-east rollout.

Notes/assumptions: - We treat the canary as "worst case" for overhead: no reliance on customer KV cache, no warm batching assumptions. - If baseline is stale or missing, we err toward limiting harder (pessimistic runtime estimate). - This PR does not change the prompt distribution; it just changes how quickly we run it.

Follow-ups: - Integrate budget inputs with control-plane config surface once PR-19203 lands in `redwood` (config validation + audit log). - Add per-SKU budget overrides once we have eu-west A10 vs H100 variance characterized (ENG-4882).

title: Fix deadlock in quality gate run cancelation + add regression test
preview: Context: During internal beta for Optimize quality gates, we hit a rare deadlock when a gate run is canceled while the eval-harness callback is concurrently finalizing results. This surfaced as gate runs stuck in RUNNING forever (no terminal state), blocking apply/rollout, and caused a near-miss where a canary pipeline sat waiting until manual intervention. Root cause is a lock ordering inversion between (a) GateRun state transitions in Optimize (DB row lock + in-memory mutex keyed by gate_run_id) and (b) cancelation handler that attempted to revoke the eval job and update state while holding the per-run mutex, while the callback path held the DB transaction and attempted to acquire the same mutex to emit completion events. Fix: remove the cross-path mutex dependency by (1) making cancelation idempotent and purely DB-driven, using a single conditional UPDATE that only transitions RUNNING->CANCEL_REQUESTED, (2) moving external side effects (eval-harness revoke + audit event emission) out of the DB transaction, and (3) ensuring the callback finalization path uses compare-and-set semantics and respects CANCEL_REQUESTED by transitioning to CANCELED (with artifacts preserved) instead of writing PASSED/FAILED. Also adds a deterministic regression test that simulates callback + cancel racing under a controllable scheduler and asserts we always reach a terminal state within a bounded time. Post-incident notes: This was first observed in eu-west during GPU scarcity where retries stretched gate duration, increasing the probability of cancel/finish overlap; see incident thread #incidents 1764369002 and support ticket SUP-18487 for timeline. This PR is a hotfix targeted for beta stability and does not change gate policy/threshold behavior.

title: Add quant profile rollback benchmarks (latency + quality) and CI artifact export
preview:
Title: Add quant profile rollback benchmarks (latency + quality) and CI artifact export

Context
We’ve had a few regressions during quant profile experiments where we needed to confirm that a rollback actually restores baseline behavior (both performance and quality) before unpausing a rollout. This PR adds first-class benchmark scenarios to benchmark-lab that (1) compare two quant profile versions head-to-head and (2) validate that a rollback from candidate -> baseline produces results within defined tolerances.

What’s in this PR
- New benchmark suite: quant_profile_rollback
  - Runs A/B comparisons for two quant profile versions (e.g., qp_v12 vs qp_v11) on the same prompt distribution and concurrency.
  - Runs an explicit rollback scenario: baseline -> candidate -> baseline, ensuring the second baseline run matches the first baseline run (within noise thresholds).
- Metrics captured
  - Perf: p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency, tokens/sec, time-to-first-token, GPU util (if exported by runner), request 5xx rate.
  - Quality: task-level aggregate metrics from the eval harness output (pass@1 / exact match where applicable), plus a lightweight “output drift” metric (normalized Levenshtein distance over a fixed deterministic subset) to detect surprising changes that still pass task thresholds.
- Artifacts
  - Emits a single JSON summary artifact suitable for safe-apply gates: artifacts/quant_profile_rollback_summary.json.
  - Includes tags for tenant/model/region/config_version when provided (for parity with the rollout health dashboard annotations).
- CI integration
  - Adds a CPU-only “smoke” mode for PR validation (schema + harness wiring + artifact format).
  - Keeps the full GPU run behind the existing nightly / on-demand workflow (no new default GPU CI spend).

How it works
- The suite takes two quant profiles and a model target:
  - --baseline-profile (required)
  - --candidate-profile (required)
  - --model (default: rw/llama-3.1-8b-instruct)
- It executes three phases in order:
  1) baseline: run prompts, record perf + quality, save output fingerprints for deterministic subset
  2) candidate: same workload, record deltas vs baseline
  3) rollback_to_baseline: re-run baseline profile and compare against phase (1)
- Pass/fail logic is intentionally conservative and mirrors the gating ADR direction:
  - rollback baseline perf must be within +3% p95 and +5% p99 latency vs initial baseline (configurable)
  - rollback baseline quality must be within 0.2% absolute of initial baseline on aggregate metric (configurable)
  - output drift on deterministic subset must not exceed threshold (defaults tuned to avoid flaking)

Usage
- Local (requires access to benchmark runner + dataset):
  - ./bench run quant_profile_rollback --model rw/llama-3.1-8b-instruct --baseline-profile qp_v11 --candidate-profile qp_v12 --concurrency 32 --prompt-set prompts/quant_canary_v3.jsonl
- CI (smoke):
  - .github/workflows/bench-smoke.yml runs with --smoke and validates summary artifact schema.
- Nightly/on-demand GPU:
  - .github/workflows/bench-quant-rollback.yml

Files / areas changed (high level)
- benches/quant_profile_rollback/*: new scenario + runners
- harness/quality/*: adapter for pulling aggregate metrics + deterministic subset drift calculation
- tools/artifacts/export_summary.py: JSON summary writer shared with other suites
- .github/workflows/bench-smoke.yml: schema smoke
- .github/workflows/bench-quant-rollback.yml: nightly/on-demand GPU execution
- docs/benchmarks/quant_profile_rollback.md: how to run + interpret output

Illustrative summary JSON (excerpt)
{
  "suite": "quant_profile_rollback",
  "model": "rw/llama-3.1-8b-instruct",
  "baseline_profile": "qp_v11",
  "candidate_profile": "qp_v12",
  "baseline": {"p95_ms": 182.4, "p99_ms": 311.7, "quality": 0.8421},
  "candidate": {"p95_ms": 175.1, "p99_ms": 298.2, "quality": 0.8416},
  "rollback_to_baseline": {"p95_ms": 183.6, "p99_ms": 314.1, "quality": 0.8420},
  "gates": {"rollback_perf_ok": true, "rollback_quality_ok": true, "drift_ok": true}
}

Notes / limitations
- This does not attempt to prove kernel-level cache invalidation correctness. It is an end-to-end benchmark meant to catch “mixed state” issues and obvious regressions after profile swaps.
- The drift metric is only evaluated on a deterministic subset (fixed seed, temp=0, top_p=1) to keep noise low.

Review discussion (summary)
- Jonas Weber: Requested that quality gate be absolute-delta, not relative, and that we record the exact eval harness version/hash in the summary.
  - Arjun Desai: Updated gates to absolute delta, added harness_git_sha in artifact metadata.
- Sana Farid: Flagged potential flake from small sample size on the deterministic subset; suggested increasing subset from 64 -> 128 and adding a minimum token count.
  - Arjun Desai: Bumped subset to 128, added min_total_tokens guard; smoke mode uses a smaller subset but only validates schema.
- Noah Patel: Asked whether rollback run should warm up separately to avoid cold-start skew.
  - Arjun Desai: Added warmup_requests per phase (default 20) and excluded warmup from metrics.
- Sean Gallagher: Asked for clear “operator reading” in docs and a single red/green output line suitable for runbooks.
  - Arjun Desai: Added docs section “What to look at during rollback” and an explicit SUMMARY: PASS/FAIL line.

CI
- bench-smoke (ubuntu-latest): PASS
- lint/format: PASS
- unit tests (harness + artifact schema): PASS
- bench-quant-rollback (on-demand GPU): PASS (attached artifact)

Merge
Squash merged. Follow-up: wire this artifact into perf-canary gate publishing (tracked separately under rollout controller integration work).

title: Optimize quality gates: add override flow w/ expiration + audit events (admin-only)
preview:
## Summary
Implements the time-bound override workflow for Optimize quality gate failures. This enables an authorized approver to temporarily override a failed gate (or a blocked apply) with an explicit reason, expiration, and an auditable trail.

Key properties:
- Overrides are **time-bound** (required `expires_at`) with max TTL enforcement.
- Only users with an **admin override role** may grant/extend/revoke overrides.
- Overrides are **scoped** (route + model + proposed config diff hash) to avoid blanket bypass.
- All override actions emit **audit events** (request/grant/deny/revoke/expire) and are queryable.
- Optimize apply and rollout promotion paths treat an active override as an explicit exception (still logged).

This PR is part of the “Quality guardrails gating for Optimize” project and lands the last missing piece needed for limited beta (see ENG-3927 / PM-482).

## Motivation
During internal beta we had legitimate cases where a gate fails due to known eval flakiness or an urgent cost/latency incident response. We need a controlled exception mechanism that:
1) does not silently bypass quality gates,
2) expires automatically,
3) has strong RBAC and auditability.

## What’s included
### 1) Data model + state machine
- Adds `quality_gate_overrides` persistence keyed by `(account_id, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash)`.
- Override state: `REQUESTED` -> `GRANTED` | `DENIED` -> `REVOKED` | `EXPIRED`.
- Enforces: `expires_at` required; `expires_at <= now + MAX_OVERRIDE_TTL` (default 72h; configurable per env).
- Supports `extend` with the same TTL cap; extension requires a new audit event.

### 2) API endpoints (Optimize service)
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:request`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:grant`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:deny`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:revoke`
- `GET  /v1/quality-gates/overrides?account_id=&route_id=&model_id=`

Notes:
- “request” can be made by route editors, but “grant/deny/revoke” requires admin override role.
- All endpoints require an explicit `reason` string and optional `ticket` reference (Linear/Jira).

### 3) Enforcement points
- Apply boundary: if `GateResult == FAILED` and no active override -> apply is blocked (existing behavior from pr-19531). If active override exists -> allow apply but mark as `OVERRIDDEN` and log.
- Rollout coupling (prep): response payload includes `override_status` so rollout controller/Console can show “allowed by override until <ts>”. (Full rollout controller enforcement remains defense-in-depth; this PR ensures Optimize-side enforcement is correct.)

### 4) Audit log events
Emits audit events via the existing audit pipeline:
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.requested`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.granted`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.denied`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.revoked`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.expired`

Event fields include: account_id, actor_id, actor_email, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash, gate_run_id (if present), expires_at, reason, and linked ticket.

### 5) Admin-only guardrails
- Introduces `RBAC_PERMISSION_OPTIMIZE_GATE_OVERRIDE_APPROVE`.
- Adds server-side checks for:
  - admin role requirement
  - max TTL
  - cannot grant an override for a config diff that fails static validation (we still block unsafe diffs)
  - cannot grant override if gate is `PENDING/RUNNING` (must have a terminal failed result or explicit “apply blocked by gate” context)

### 6) Background expiration
- Adds a lightweight sweeper job (`override_expirer`) that marks overrides as expired and emits `...expired` audit event.
- Expiration is also enforced on read paths (i.e., an override past expiration is treated as inactive even if sweeper is delayed).

## Files/areas changed (high level)
- `services/optimize/api/quality_gates/override_endpoints.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/override_service.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/enforcement.ts`
- `services/optimize/db/migrations/20260122_add_quality_gate_overrides.sql`
- `services/optimize/rbac/permissions.ts`
- `services/optimize/audit/events/quality_gate_override_events.ts`
- `services/optimize/jobs/override_expirer.ts`
- `services/optimize/tests/quality_gates/override_flow.test.ts`

## API response changes
- Gate summary responses now include `override`: `{ status, expires_at, granted_by, granted_at }` (flattened into existing response fields; no breaking change for older clients).
- Apply response includes `applied_by_override=true/false` and `override_id` when relevant.

## Testing
- Unit tests: TTL enforcement, RBAC checks, scoping, and expiration behavior.
- Integration tests: request -> grant -> apply allowed -> audit events emitted; revoke -> apply blocked.
- Added regression test for “expired override treated as inactive even if record exists”.

## Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag `optimize_quality_gate_overrides_enabled` (on by default in staging, off in prod until limited beta).
- Admin permission assignment tracked via INT-3391.

## Follow-ups
- Console UI wiring for override request/grant prompts is tracked in ENG-3925 / DES-611.
- Export of these audit events to customer-facing audit log UI is tracked separately (Console milestone).

text:
## Summary
Implements the time-bound override workflow for Optimize quality gate failures. This enables an authorized approver to temporarily override a failed gate (or a blocked apply) with an explicit reason, expiration, and an auditable trail.

Key properties:
- Overrides are **time-bound** (required `expires_at`) with max TTL enforcement.
- Only users with an **admin override role** may grant/extend/revoke overrides.
- Overrides are **scoped** (route + model + proposed config diff hash) to avoid blanket bypass.
- All override actions emit **audit events** (request/grant/deny/revoke/expire) and are queryable.
- Optimize apply and rollout promotion paths treat an active override as an explicit exception (still logged).

This PR is part of the “Quality guardrails gating for Optimize” project and lands the last missing piece needed for limited beta (see ENG-3927 / PM-482).

## Motivation
During internal beta we had legitimate cases where a gate fails due to known eval flakiness or an urgent cost/latency incident response. We need a controlled exception mechanism that:
1) does not silently bypass quality gates,
2) expires automatically,
3) has strong RBAC and auditability.

## What’s included
### 1) Data model + state machine
- Adds `quality_gate_overrides` persistence keyed by `(account_id, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash)`.
- Override state: `REQUESTED` -> `GRANTED` | `DENIED` -> `REVOKED` | `EXPIRED`.
- Enforces: `expires_at` required; `expires_at <= now + MAX_OVERRIDE_TTL` (default 72h; configurable per env).
- Supports `extend` with the same TTL cap; extension requires a new audit event.

### 2) API endpoints (Optimize service)
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:request`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:grant`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:deny`
- `POST /v1/quality-gates/overrides:revoke`
- `GET  /v1/quality-gates/overrides?account_id=&route_id=&model_id=`

Notes:
- “request” can be made by route editors, but “grant/deny/revoke” requires admin override role.
- All endpoints require an explicit `reason` string and optional `ticket` reference (Linear/Jira).

### 3) Enforcement points
- Apply boundary: if `GateResult == FAILED` and no active override -> apply is blocked (existing behavior from pr-19531). If active override exists -> allow apply but mark as `OVERRIDDEN` and log.
- Rollout coupling (prep): response payload includes `override_status` so rollout controller/Console can show “allowed by override until <ts>”. (Full rollout controller enforcement remains defense-in-depth; this PR ensures Optimize-side enforcement is correct.)

### 4) Audit log events
Emits audit events via the existing audit pipeline:
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.requested`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.granted`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.denied`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.revoked`
- `optimize.quality_gate.override.expired`

Event fields include: account_id, actor_id, actor_email, route_id, model_id, config_diff_hash, gate_run_id (if present), expires_at, reason, and linked ticket.

### 5) Admin-only guardrails
- Introduces `RBAC_PERMISSION_OPTIMIZE_GATE_OVERRIDE_APPROVE`.
- Adds server-side checks for:
  - admin role requirement
  - max TTL
  - cannot grant an override for a config diff that fails static validation (we still block unsafe diffs)
  - cannot grant override if gate is `PENDING/RUNNING` (must have a terminal failed result or explicit “apply blocked by gate” context)

### 6) Background expiration
- Adds a lightweight sweeper job (`override_expirer`) that marks overrides as expired and emits `...expired` audit event.
- Expiration is also enforced on read paths (i.e., an override past expiration is treated as inactive even if sweeper is delayed).

## Files/areas changed (high level)
- `services/optimize/api/quality_gates/override_endpoints.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/override_service.ts`
- `services/optimize/core/quality_gates/enforcement.ts`
- `services/optimize/db/migrations/20260122_add_quality_gate_overrides.sql`
- `services/optimize/rbac/permissions.ts`
- `services/optimize/audit/events/quality_gate_override_events.ts`
- `services/optimize/jobs/override_expirer.ts`
- `services/optimize/tests/quality_gates/override_flow.test.ts`

## API response changes
- Gate summary responses now include `override`: `{ status, expires_at, granted_by, granted_at }` (flattened into existing response fields; no breaking change for older clients).
- Apply response includes `applied_by_override=true/false` and `override_id` when relevant.

## Testing
- Unit tests: TTL enforcement, RBAC checks, scoping, and expiration behavior.
- Integration tests: request -> grant -> apply allowed -> audit events emitted; revoke -> apply blocked.
- Added regression test for “expired override treated as inactive even if record exists”.

## Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag `optimize_quality_gate_overrides_enabled` (on by default in staging, off in prod until limited beta).
- Admin permission assignment tracked via INT-3391.

## Follow-ups
- Console UI wiring for override request/grant prompts is tracked in ENG-3925 / DES-611.
- Export of these audit events to customer-facing audit log UI is tracked separately (Console milestone).

title: Add gating metrics emission and tracing
preview: This PR instruments the Optimize quality-gate execution path with (1) low-cardinality metrics for funnel + latency + retry/timeout visibility, (2) OpenTelemetry tracing spans across Optimize -> eval-harness -> persistence -> policy enforcement, and (3) structured logs to make it possible to debug why a gate run failed/was retried/was blocked without needing to scrape application logs. Motivation: during the internal beta we’ve had multiple “it failed but we don’t know where” cases (eval-harness timeouts vs persistence retries vs policy-block) and we need consistent signals before we expand to limited production rollout. Scope: metrics, traces, and logs for gate runs, plus guardrails around tag cardinality and PII. Non-goals: dashboards/alerts (tracked in observability-pack PR) and any changes to gating pass/fail logic. Implementation details: Adds a top-level span `quality_gate.run` with child spans for baseline selection, config-diff validation, eval-harness trigger, result aggregation, and apply-block enforcement. Adds counters + histograms under `redwood.optimize.quality_gates.*` and emits one final structured log per gate run completion with stable reason codes. Metrics include: started/completed (by status), duration, eval-harness latency, retry count, timeout count, and flake_score distribution (if present). Tag set is intentionally constrained: `model_family`, `config_type` (batching|cache|quant), `gate_tier` (smoke|full), `status`, `failure_reason` (enum), `region`, `route_tier` (self_serve|enterprise), `runtime_sku` (coarse), and `eval_harness_version`. We do NOT tag by route_id, account_id, prompt_set_id, or model_version to avoid cardinality blowups; those are instead included in structured logs (hashed/shortened) and trace attributes behind sampling. Adds log redaction for any user-provided config payloads; logs only diff summary + validated knob names. Includes unit tests for reason-code mapping + a small integration test asserting we emit spans/metrics on a forced timeout path.

title: Console: add rollback trigger endpoint with server-side confirmation checks
preview:
Adds the Console backend endpoint used by the new “Rollback” button for Optimization Config versions. This PR focuses on server-side confirmation/guardrails so the Console UI can remain thin and we avoid dangerous client-only checks.

Key behavior
- New endpoint: POST /internal/console/optimization-config/rollbacks
  - Triggers a rollback for a given scope (tenant + model + region) to a target config_version_id.
  - Returns a rollback_operation_id and the initial operation state.
- Confirmation model
  - Requires a confirmation payload computed server-side and echoed back by the client (type-to-confirm UX can be driven from this).
  - The server recomputes the confirmation on request and rejects if it doesn’t match (prevents stale modal confirmations).
- Guardrails (server-enforced)
  - Blast-radius summary + limits: rejects requests that exceed allowed scope cardinality for Console-triggered rollback (e.g., wildcard tenant + wildcard model).
  - Cooldown: prevents repeated rollback thrash for the same scope within a configurable window (default 15m) unless caller has override permission.
  - Safety checks: disallow rollback while an apply operation is actively progressing for the same scope (must pause/cancel first).
  - Version checks: cannot rollback to a version that is not in the “promoted/known-good” state unless override is present.
  - Idempotency: supports optional Idempotency-Key header to avoid duplicate rollback operations on retries.
- RBAC / audit
  - Enforces permission optimization_config.rollback at the endpoint.
  - Emits audit events: optimization_config.rollback_requested and optimization_config.rollback_rejected with standardized fields (request_id, actor, scope, from_version, to_version, reason, confirmation_hash).

API details
- Request fields (high level)
  - scope: tenant_id, model_id, region (no wildcards from Console path)
  - target_version_id
  - reason (required; displayed in history/audit)
  - confirmation: { summary_hash, typed_phrase } (typed_phrase is validated against the server-provided phrase)
- Response fields (high level)
  - rollback_operation_id
  - state: queued|running|completed|failed
  - active_version_before
  - target_version
  - effective_cooldown_until (when applicable)

Implementation notes
- Confirmation computation lives in ConsoleRollbackConfirmationService and includes: scope display name(s), current active version, target version, and a deterministic hash of {scope, from, to, cooldown_bucket}.
- Cooldown is stored in control plane as a scope-keyed record with TTL; implemented with a compare-and-set to avoid race conditions under concurrent requests.
- Conflict detection uses the existing rollout/apply operation table: if there is a non-terminal apply operation for the same scope, rollback is rejected with HTTP 409 and a machine-readable error code APPLY_IN_PROGRESS.
- Keeps the endpoint under /internal/console/* for now; public rollback API remains in the control plane service surface (tracked separately) and will call into the same underlying RollbackOrchestrator.

Testing
- Unit tests cover confirmation mismatch, blast-radius rejection, cooldown enforcement, and idempotency.
- Integration tests cover 409 on apply-in-progress and a happy-path rollback request emitting audit events.

Rollout
- Endpoint is feature-flagged (console_optimization_rollback_enabled) and returns 404 when disabled to avoid exposing partial functionality.

Follow-ups
- Hook up Console UI to show effective_cooldown_until and suggested next action (“wait” vs “request override”).
- Add support for Dedicated “shard” scopes once shard-based rollouts land (ENG-4824).

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4828, ENG-4825
- Related audit taxonomy work: ENG-4826
- Addresses internal on-call confusion from INT-3390 by making permission errors explicit and returning required_permission in the error payload.

title: Add fast smoke-gates tier for batching/cache Optimize changes
preview: Context: We’re adding mandatory quality guardrails before Optimize can apply cost-saving config changes (batching policy, prefix/KV caching). Full gate runs are comprehensive but can take 30–60 min and are expensive; teams need a faster precheck to catch obvious regressions and provide tight feedback loops during iteration. This PR introduces a new “smoke gates” tier that runs a small, high-signal prompt subset before scheduling full gates. Scope: smoke gates are enabled only for batching + caching config diffs (NOT quantization profile changes) and only in hosted/staging initially. Summary of changes: (1) Gate tier model: Introduces GateTier=SMOKE|FULL and stores tier on GateRun. A single Optimize recommendation/apply attempt now creates a GateRunGroup with an ordered plan (SMOKE -> FULL). (2) Prompt subset selection: Adds a new eval-harness request mode specifying prompt_set_id plus a “subset” selector. For smoke tier we use curated “gating_smoke_v1” subset (e.g., ~80 prompts) that is intentionally skewed toward known sensitivity cases for batching/caching (streaming boundaries, long-context prefix reuse, tool-call formatting). (3) Thresholds: Smoke tier uses stricter but simpler pass/fail logic to minimize false negatives: it blocks on (a) task_success drop beyond -0.5pp absolute OR (b) rubric_mean drop beyond -0.03, using a minimum sample size and a single rerun on variance spikes. Full tier keeps the v1 thresholds/variance model and is still required for any apply. (4) Scheduling + time bounds: Adds tier-specific SLAs and timeouts (smoke target <10 min, hard timeout 15 min; full unchanged). Implements a “fast-fail” policy: if smoke FAILS, we do not queue full. If smoke PASSES, we enqueue full automatically. (5) Fallback behavior: If smoke cannot produce a decision due to infra reasons (GPU capacity / eval-harness 5xx / timeout) we mark SMOKE=INCONCLUSIVE with reason_code and continue to FULL (so we don’t block rollout pipelines on smoke flakiness). If smoke is INCONCLUSIVE due to variance/flakiness (high flake_score or repeated inconclusive after rerun), we also proceed to FULL but emit a metric and annotate the Console summary to discourage relying on the smoke result. (6) Config + feature flags: Adds route-tier policy fields to gating policy config: smoke_enabled (default true for batching/cache), smoke_prompt_set=gating_smoke_v1, smoke_timeout_seconds, and a global kill-switch optimize.gates.smoke.disable for emergency rollback. Also adds allow_smoke_inconclusive_to_full (default true) to prevent accidental blocking during capacity events. (7) Observability: Emits gate_run_count and gate_run_latency_ms tagged by tier, plus smoke_inconclusive_total with reason_code. Adds a trace span “quality_gate.smoke” around the smoke execution path with eval_run_id linking to artifacts. (8) API behavior: The existing GateRun summary endpoint now returns “current_tier” and “plan” to support Console UX (Console work follows in ENG-3925). Apply remains blocked until FULL passes (or an override is granted) per PR-19531/PR-19537; smoke tier does not change enforcement semantics, only sequencing and time-to-signal. Test plan: Unit tests for plan construction (by config diff type), state machine transitions for SMOKE->FULL, and fallback handling; integration tests against eval-harness stub for timeout/5xx behavior; load test in staging to verify smoke tier reduces full-gate queue pressure on iteration workflows. Rollout plan: Enable smoke tier in staging immediately; enable for hosted production routes behind feature flag for internal accounts first, then expand during limited beta once we see stable inconclusive rate (<3%) and median smoke duration <8 min. Risks/notes: Primary risk is false positives due to subset skew; mitigated by allowing infra/variance inconclusive to proceed to full and by not allowing smoke to ever “greenlight” an apply. Coordination: Aligns with ENG-3923 and depends on the eval-harness trigger endpoint (PR-412) supporting subset selectors; if eval-harness does not recognize subset selector it returns 400 and we treat as INCONCLUSIVE(invalid_request) and proceed to FULL to avoid deadlocks. Related: PR-19502 (gate state machine), PR-19523 (wiring + retries), PR-19511 (config diff schema), PR-19531 (apply blocking).

title: Add log redaction + no-PII assertions for perf-canary request/telemetry path
preview: Motivation: perf-canary must never emit or persist customer data. While the canary uses synthetic prompts, we still had a few sharp edges where (a) debug logging could print request bodies on errors and (b) some error/reporting paths would include raw prompt text / user-provided identifiers if they ever slipped through config. This PR adds explicit redaction + hard assertions so we fail-closed and keep logs/metrics safe. Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a centralized redaction helper for log fields and error payloads; (2) Add runtime assertions that enforce "synthetic-only" prompt sources (no external prompt injection), with an opt-out only for local dev; (3) Ensure we never log request bodies, prompt strings, or headers that may contain identifiers; (4) Add "safe fields" allowlist for alert webhook payloads so incident-bot never receives prompt content; (5) Add tests that simulate error paths (HTTP 4xx/5xx, timeouts, JSON decode errors) and verify log output + alert payload are redacted; (6) Add lightweight "no_pii" static checks on structured logging calls in canary request loop. Operational notes: if assertions trip in prod, the canary request will be dropped and counted under canary_safety_dropped_requests_total with reason=no_pii_guard; the process stays healthy and continues other model targets. Checklist: [x] No prompt strings in logs on success/error paths [x] No customer identifiers in metrics labels [x] Alert payload contains only model/region/version/baseline/observed deltas + links [x] Unit tests for redaction + guardrails [x] Docs note in README about dev-only override. Risk: low/medium; touches error handling and logging. Rollout: safe to deploy alongside existing canary; no config change required. Follow-ups: add a prometheus metric for redaction_invocations_total to track unexpected surfaces; consider moving dev-only override behind a build tag.

Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a centralized redaction helper for log fields and error payloads; (2) Add runtime assertions that enforce "synthetic-only" prompt sources (no external prompt injection), with an opt-out only for local dev; (3) Ensure we never log request bodies, prompt strings, or headers that may contain identifiers; (4) Add "safe fields" allowlist for alert webhook payloads so incident-bot never receives prompt content; (5) Add tests that simulate error paths (HTTP 4xx/5xx, timeouts, JSON decode errors) and verify log output + alert payload are redacted; (6) Add lightweight "no_pii" static checks on structured logging calls in canary request loop. Operational notes: if assertions trip in prod, the canary request will be dropped and counted under canary_safety_dropped_requests_total with reason=no_pii_guard; the process stays healthy and continues other model targets. Checklist: [x] No prompt strings in logs on success/error paths [x] No customer identifiers in metrics labels [x] Alert payload contains only model/region/version/baseline/observed deltas + links [x] Unit tests for redaction + guardrails [x] Docs note in README about dev-only override. Risk: low/medium; touches error handling and logging. Rollout: safe to deploy alongside existing canary; no config change required. Follow-ups: add a prometheus metric for redaction_invocations_total to track unexpected surfaces; consider moving dev-only override behind a build tag.

title: Add internal perf-canary auth scope + restricted service account for inference reads
preview:
Context: The perf-canary service (see perf-canary repo PRs) needs to call internal inference endpoints continuously from prod. We do not want to reuse broad internal scopes (or customer-style API keys) because (a) blast radius is too large, (b) auditing is noisy, and (c) it complicates incident response when we need to quickly disable only canary traffic. This PR introduces a restricted auth scope + service account intended solely for synthetic canary inference reads.

What’s in this PR:
- New auth scope: `inference.canary:read` (internal-only). This scope can only be minted for service accounts (not user tokens) and is denied in external auth flows.
- New service account type + default policy bundle: `svc-perf-canary` (control-plane managed). The policy allows:
  - POST `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/completions` against internal inference gateways
  - streaming responses
  - read-only access to model metadata needed for request normalization (canonical model + version)
  And explicitly denies:
  - any admin/control-plane routes
  - any write/update routes (model registry writes, routing config, quotas)
  - any endpoint that could return customer prompts/logs
- Gateway enforcement: inference gateway now recognizes `inference.canary:read` and applies additional constraints:
  - requires `X-Redwood-Canary: true` header (prevents accidental reuse)
  - requires `X-Redwood-Caller: perf-canary` (auditing/dedupe key)
  - enforces per-request caps: `max_tokens <= 512`, `n == 1`, `logprobs == false`, `store == false`
  - disables tool/function calling for canary scope (reduces surface area + variance)
- Audit logging: adds structured auth/audit fields `caller_type=service_account`, `service_account=svc-perf-canary`, `auth_scope=inference.canary:read` on inference gateway logs so we can correlate canary-driven load and quickly validate that the canary is not calling broader routes.
- Documentation: updates internal auth scope registry docs with ownership + rotation expectations for this scope.

Why this design:
- Least privilege: canary needs “generate tokens” but should not be able to mutate config or read any customer data.
- Fast kill-switch: we can revoke the service account or deny-list the scope at the gateway without impacting other internal tooling.
- Predictable observability: dedicated scope + headers make it easy to filter canary traffic from production dashboards and to attach clear attribution during incidents.

Operational notes:
- This PR does not create the service account secret/IAM bindings; that is wired in terraform (tracked under INT-2213 and ENG-4879). This PR only adds the scope, gateway enforcement, and policy plumbing.
- Backwards compatibility: existing internal inference callers are unchanged. The new scope is additive.

Test plan:
- Unit: added auth policy evaluation tests ensuring `svc-perf-canary` cannot access denied routes and cannot exceed request caps.
- Integration (staging): exercised canary token against internal gateway with synthetic requests; verified 200s on allowed routes and 403s/422s on denied/invalid requests.
- Observability: verified audit log fields present and that `X-Redwood-Canary` traffic is filterable in log explorer.

Rollout:
1) Merge this PR.
2) Apply terraform to create `svc-perf-canary` and mint a token restricted to `inference.canary:read`.
3) Deploy perf-canary with new token + required headers.
4) Monitor `inference_gateway_authz_denied_total{scope="inference.canary:read"}` and request volume for 24h.

Notes for reviewers:
- Policy lives under `authz/policies/service_accounts/perf_canary.yaml`.
- Gateway constraints are intentionally conservative to reduce both variance and abuse potential. If we need tool calling later for coverage, we should add a separate scope rather than expanding this one.

Related:
- perf-canary service skeleton: sources/github/perf-canary/pr-19201-bootstrap-perf-canary-service-skeleton.json
- Control plane config surface for canary targets: PR-19203
- Service account + secrets wiring (terraform): tracked under INT-2213 / ENG-4879

title: Embeddings: add high-QPS mode + throughput-first report + percentile latency export
preview:
Summary: Adds an embeddings workload mode designed for high-QPS / small-payload traffic shapes and a throughput-first reporting path (req/s + embeddings/s, plus tokens/s where applicable). Also exports percentile latency (p50/p95/p99) in machine-readable form for perf-canary + dashboard ingestion. This is needed for Q1 2026 baseline sweeps because embeddings traffic is dominated by concurrency/queueing behavior and req/s saturation, and the existing generator/reporting was text-generation-centric (TTFT/TPOT) and didn’t provide stable throughput/saturation indicators.

Motivation / context: Our representative workload suite v1 includes an embeddings cohort with production-like payload sizes and concurrency targets (high QPS, small input lengths, mostly non-streaming). For baseline comparability we need: (1) a generator that can drive stable, controlled offered load (open-loop) while still supporting closed-loop for latency-first runs, (2) a report that prioritizes throughput and saturation detection (req/s, embeddings/s, error rate, queue wait) over TTFT/TPOT, and (3) percentile export in the canonical results schema (ADR-0172) so SRE/obs can build consistent dashboards and gating thresholds.

What’s in this PR (high level): 1) New workload type: `embeddings_high_qps` - Adds a workload config + generator that issues embeddings requests with configurable input length distributions and concurrency/QPS targets. - Supports both "closed-loop" (N concurrent workers, measure latency) and "open-loop" (target QPS, measure achieved throughput + latency under load). - Adds a warmup stage and steady-state windows to reduce variance (warmup defaults to 60s; measurement defaults to 10m; both configurable).

2) Throughput-first report: `ThroughputReport` - Outputs throughput as: - `requests_per_second` (primary) - `embeddings_per_second` (alias of rps; kept for readability in baseline tables) - `input_tokens_per_second` (optional; only when tokenizer/model metadata is available) - Adds saturation signals: in-flight, queue depth (if provided by server metrics), and client-side backpressure counters.

3) Percentile latency export: - Computes latency percentiles over the steady-state window and writes: - JSON results artifact (`results.json`) aligned with the run manifest id - CSV summary (`summary.csv`) for quick spreadsheet comparisons - Percentiles included: p50/p90/p95/p99 plus min/max; units in milliseconds; explicit measurement boundaries (client wall clock from request send to response fully received).

4) Results schema alignment + tagging: - Results include the same top-level tags as other workloads: model, region, sku, runtime_build, workload_name, seed, and scenario (cold vs warm cache). - Adds `workload_mode=open_loop|closed_loop` and `target_qps` to allow apples-to-apples comparisons and gating.

CLI / usage: - New flags (all optional; defaults tuned for baseline runs): - `--workload embeddings_high_qps` - `--mode open_loop|closed_loop` (default: open_loop) - `--target-qps <float>` (open_loop only) - `--concurrency <int>` (closed_loop only) - `--input-len-dist <preset|path>` (default: preset "prod_p50_small") - `--warmup-seconds <int>` (default: 60) - `--measurement-seconds <int>` (default: 600) - `--export-results-json <path>` and `--export-summary-csv <path>`

Notes on tokens/s: For embeddings, “tokens/s” is ambiguous and easy to misuse (no decode phase; tokenizer choice affects counts). This PR reports `input_tokens_per_second` only when: (a) the model config includes a tokenizer id, (b) tokenization is enabled for the run, and (c) the tokenization version is captured in the run manifest. Otherwise the report emits `input_tokens_per_second=null` and logs a one-line note. The primary baseline metric for embeddings remains req/s + latency percentiles.

Testing / validation: - Added unit tests for: - percentile computation (HDR histogram rounding behavior; verifies p99) - open-loop scheduler stability (target QPS within ±2% in deterministic mode) - schema validation: required fields present; rejects missing tags for baseline runs - Added an e2e smoke test behind CI label `gpu-optional` that runs against the mock server and ensures JSON/CSV artifacts are created.

Changed areas (files): - `cmd/bench/main.py`: adds embeddings workload wiring + flags - `bench/workloads/embeddings_high_qps.py`: new generator + pacing strategies - `bench/reporting/throughput_report.py`: new report + exporters - `bench/results/schema.py`: adds optional throughput fields + `workload_mode` tag - `bench/tests/test_percentiles.py`: percentile export correctness - `docs/workloads/embeddings.md`: new docs section and recommended baseline settings

Example output (trimmed): - `summary.csv` (steady-state): `workload,mode,target_qps,achieved_rps,p50_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms,error_rate` -> `embeddings_high_qps,open_loop,1500,1492.6,18.4,32.1,57.8,0.0003`

Review notes / decisions: - Measurement boundary: client wall-clock (send -> full response received). This matches other benchmark-lab workloads and is consistent with our canary comparisons; we explicitly do NOT attempt to subtract client overhead beyond standard connection reuse. - Open-loop default: chosen for embeddings because it surfaces saturation and avoids hiding queueing by throttling at concurrency limits; closed-loop still supported for latency-only investigations.

Follow-ups (not in scope): - Hook in optional server-side queue wait metric collection from runtime instrumentation (ENG-4826) once it lands in the runtime and is plumbed into benchmark-lab collectors. - Add per-request size tagging to quantify payload-size impact under mixed distributions.

Checklist: - [x] Linked Linear (ENG-4825, ENG-4822) - [x] Docs updated - [x] Results JSON validated against schema - [x] Unit tests added - [x] CI green - [x] Added example baseline config file

Commits (squash summary): - Add embeddings_high_qps workload generator with open-loop pacing - Add throughput-first reporting + JSON/CSV exporters - Align results with manifest tagging + schema updates - Tests + docs

CI: - benchmark-lab / unit-tests: PASS - benchmark-lab / lint: PASS - benchmark-lab / schema-validate: PASS - benchmark-lab / e2e-mockserver: PASS - benchmark-lab / gpu-optional-smoke: SKIPPED on PR (ran manually, attached logs)

Review conversation (highlights): - Claire Dubois: "Can we make it explicit that percentiles are steady-state only (excluding warmup)? Also please add p90." -> Rina: Added `window=steady_state` tag and added p90 to exports; docs now state warmup exclusion. - Sana Farid: "Open-loop pacing: do we have guardrails so we don’t claim throughput when we’re actually dropping/429’ing?" -> Rina: Added error_rate to the report and a `throughput_effective_rps` field (achieved_rps * (1 - error_rate)) used in gating recommendations; also added a hard warning when error_rate > 0.5%. - Sean Gallagher: "CSV is helpful but dashboards want JSON; ensure stable field names and units." -> Rina: Confirmed ms units everywhere, added `*_ms` suffix fields, and pinned schema version in results artifact. - Jonas Weber: "Please add a note about tokenizer versioning so quality/perf analyses don’t get confused." -> Rina: Added `tokenizer_id` and `tokenizer_version` to manifest requirements for token reporting; tokens/s is omitted if not present.

Outcome: Merged (squash). This unblocks embeddings baseline sweeps and perf-canary ingestion for the embeddings cohort.
