title: runtime: add KV allocator fragmentation counters + export hooks
preview: Context: We’ve been chasing burst-driven p95/p99 spikes that correlate with KV arena churn and suspected allocator fragmentation. Before we can tune compaction/eviction, we need allocator-level signals that are (a) cheap, (b) interpretable, and (c) consistent across Hosted/Dedicated. This PR adds a small set of fragmentation/health counters/gauges emitted by the KV cache allocator, and wires them into the standard runtime metrics pipeline so SRE/Perf Canary can build guardrails and dashboards. Metrics added (names are final, definitions in code comments): kv_allocator_free_bytes (gauge): total free bytes in KV arena; kv_allocator_used_bytes (gauge): total used bytes in KV arena; kv_allocator_largest_free_block_bytes (gauge): size of largest contiguous free block (best-effort from free-list); kv_allocator_fragmentation_ratio (gauge): 1 - (largest_free_block_bytes / max(free_bytes, 1)); kv_allocator_alloc_failures_total (counter): allocation failures (pre-OOM); kv_allocator_defrag_slowpath_total (counter): times we had to take slow path to satisfy request due to fragmentation; kv_allocator_free_list_nodes (gauge): number of nodes in free-list (proxy for fragmentation/overhead). Implementation notes: Fragmentation ratio is computed from allocator bookkeeping (no extra scans beyond what we already track for coalescing). Largest-free-block is updated on free/alloc/coalesce operations; we avoid O(n) iteration per request. All updates are behind a lightweight lock already held in the allocator critical section; exported values are read via atomic snapshot to avoid lock contention on scrape. Export is via the existing runtime metrics registry (Prometheus + OTel bridge); names are consistent with our performance standards and use base units (bytes). Dashboard wiring: Adds panels to the internal ‘runtime / kv cache’ dashboard template (initial wiring only; full rollout and alert thresholds are tracked in ENG-4830 and SRE guardrails doc). Adds exemplars tags (model, pool, tier) consistent with other runtime metrics. Testing: Adds unit tests for fragmentation ratio bounds [0,1], largest_free <= free_bytes, and alloc failure counter increments on synthetic out-of-space conditions. Also adds a microbench to ensure metric updates do not regress allocator hot path (>0.5% regression threshold). Rollout: Safe to ship with metrics on by default; no behavior change to allocation decisions. This PR is a prerequisite for compaction worker + scheduler budgeting (ENG-4823/ENG-4824).

title: Add long-context prefill workload + standardized warmup to reduce variance
preview:
Title: Add long-context prefill workload + standardized warmup to reduce variance

Context
We need a production-representative workload that stresses prefill (large prompt / long context) with modest decode, and we need more repeatable runs for the Q1 2026 baseline sweeps. In early dry runs we saw high variance driven by (a) first-run JIT + memory pool ramp, (b) GPU clocks/ramp state, and (c) caching artifacts depending on prior suite order. This PR introduces (1) a long-context prefill workload generator and (2) a standardized warmup phase that is applied consistently across workloads (opt-in in config, default-on for long_context_prefill).

What’s in this PR
1) New workload: long_context_prefill
- Goal: mimic long-context summarization / doc QA traffic: large prefill, smaller decode.
- Shape:
  - prompt length bins (tokens): 8k / 16k / 32k (configurable; default mix 0.45/0.45/0.10)
  - max_output_tokens: 256 (default; configurable)
  - streaming: off by default for this workload (we want clean TTFT + TPOT without client read pacing effects)
  - concurrency: target 1/2/4/8 ramps (default 4)
  - request inter-arrival: closed-loop (per-worker) with optional think-time jitter
- Metrics exported (same schema as suite): p50/p95/p99 TTFT, prefill_ms (server-side), decode_tpot_ms, tokens/sec, req/sec, and saturation hints (queue_wait_ms where available).
- Notes:
  - Adds explicit tracking for “prefill-only time” vs end-to-first-token (TTFT). TTFT remains canonical, but prefill_ms is exported for attribution when decode is small.

2) Standardized warmup procedure
- Adds warmup config to harness:
  - warmup.enabled (default true when workload.long_context_prefill is in the run)
  - warmup.iterations_per_concurrency (default 3)
  - warmup.discard_first (default true)
  - warmup.mode: "allocator+jit" (default), "full" (includes a short sustained run to stabilize clocks), or "none"
  - warmup.reset_kv_cache (default true) and warmup.reset_prefix_cache (default true)
- The warmup phase runs before measurement for each (model, region, sku, workload) tuple and for each concurrency level, so we don’t accidentally compare a “cold” c=1 run against a “warm” c=8 run.
- Output artifacts now include a warmup_summary.json so we can debug whether warmup actually executed (and how long it took).

3) Docs + examples
- New doc: docs/workloads/long_context_prefill.md
- Updated: docs/warmup.md to describe modes and the rationale (variance reduction + consistent measurement boundary).
- Added an example run config and sample output under examples/.

Example
Command:
  benchlab run --config configs/suite/long_context_prefill_default.yaml --model llama-3.1-70b-instruct --region us-east --gpu h100 --out artifacts/2026-01-10_longprefill_llama70b_h100_use1

Sample (trimmed) result row:
  workload=long_context_prefill concurrency=4 ttft_p50_ms=182 ttft_p99_ms=411 prefill_p50_ms=148 decode_tpot_p50_ms=33 tokens_per_sec=1080

Why this approach
- This workload captures the real tail-latency pain point we see in production cohorts dominated by large prefills: attention prefill throughput + KV cache pressure + queueing/backpressure behavior.
- Warmup standardizes the “starting state” enough to tighten confidence intervals for regression gating, without hiding real regressions (warmup is consistent across baseline and candidate builds; measured window remains the same).

- workloads/long_context_prefill.py: generator + token length sampling + closed-loop driver
- harness/warmup.py: warmup orchestration and summary artifact
- harness/run.py: integrates warmup into run graph and ensures manifests record warmup settings
- configs/suite/long_context_prefill_default.yaml: default mix + concurrency ramps
- docs/workloads/long_context_prefill.md
- docs/warmup.md
- examples/long_context_prefill/: sample config + a small golden JSON for schema sanity

- [x] Adds workload definition + config
- [x] Exports metrics to standard results schema
- [x] Captures warmup config in run manifest
- [x] Adds example run + docs
- [x] CI updated with a short-run smoke test (reduced lengths) to keep runtime reasonable

Open questions / follow-ups
- We may want to add an optional “streaming prefill” variant later (prefill heavy + stream enabled) to measure first-token-on-wire separately. Out of scope for v1 baseline.
- If SRE wants additional fleet-stability knobs (clock locking) we can document those as recommended host settings, but we should avoid baking them into harness logic.

Review conversation (highlights)
Claire Dubois (requested changes): Please be explicit in docs about measurement boundaries: TTFT is end-to-first-token from server perspective; prefill_ms is model-side prefill measurement from inside the server, so it should not be compared to client-observed TTFT. Also add a note on why streaming is off by default.

Rina Kobayashi: Added workloads/long_context_prefill_smoke.yaml and wired into ci/perf_smoke.sh; it runs <4 minutes on CI runner GPUs.

Jonas Weber (approval): Looks good. The prompt-length bins match the latest telemetry bins we discussed, and the warmup makes the noise model work simpler. Approved once CI is green.

- benchmark-lab / lint: pass
- benchmark-lab / perf-smoke (short long_context_prefill): pass
- benchmark-lab / schema-validate: pass

Timeline

- 2026-01-10: Warmup manifest + cache reset changes added after review
- 2026-01-13: CI green, approved, squashed and merged

Merge
Merged via squash into main. Commit message: "Add long-context prefill workload and standardized warmup". This PR is expected to be used in the first baseline sweeps for ENG-4824 starting this week.

title: perf-canary: add optimization config safeguards + gate signal publisher for latency regressions
preview:
Title: perf-canary: add optimization config safeguards + gate signal publisher for latency regressions

Context
Safe apply + rollback for Optimization Configs needs an external, repeatable “canary judge” that can detect config-induced latency regressions early and produce machine-readable gate signals. Today, on-call relies on dashboards + manual judgement, which slows progression and increases risk (see internal incident notes around p95 spikes after caching/batching tweaks).

What this PR does
- Adds a new scheduled/on-demand canary workflow ("optimization-safeguard") that:
  - Detects active Optimization Config rollout scopes (tenant/model/region + canary %) and config_version.
  - Pulls baseline vs canary metrics (p95/p99 latency + 5xx rate) for the last N minutes, with warmup handling.
  - Evaluates against configurable thresholds with sane defaults (latency regression % + absolute ms guard, and error-rate delta).
  - Publishes gate signals to the control-plane gate ingest endpoint so the rollout controller can pause/fail a stage.
- Standardizes metric tagging requirements used by the gates: tenant_id, model, region, route, config_version, stage_id.
- Produces a clear human-readable summary in the job logs for on-call (what scope, what changed, baseline vs canary values, and decision).

Design notes / decisions
- Windowing: uses a rolling 10m evaluation window with a 3m warmup skip after stage start to reduce false positives from cache churn. Stage start time is fetched from the rollout controller (if unavailable, falls back to “first seen” timestamp for config_version).
- Baseline selection: baseline is “same tenant/model/region, previous stable config_version” (prefer stage.previous_version; else the most recent version marked stable in history). Baseline comparisons require minimum sample sizes (req_count >= 2,000 or token_count >= 3,000,000) to avoid noisy results.
- Threshold defaults (overridable per environment):
  - p95: fail if +12% and +20ms (both) vs baseline
  - p99: fail if +15% and +35ms (both) vs baseline
  - 5xx: fail if +0.15% absolute vs baseline or > 0.50% absolute
- Publishing semantics: the job emits a single gate evaluation per (stage_id, scope) and is idempotent; repeated runs overwrite the same key to avoid “gate spam”.

Implementation overview
- New job runner: cmd/optimization_safeguard_canary (Go) with a single entrypoint supporting both cron + manual trigger.
- New query package: internal/metrics/queries/optimization_rollout.go
  - Uses PromQL (for prod) and a local stub backend for unit tests.
  - Explicitly filters by config_version for canary and baseline.
- New gate publisher: internal/gates/publisher.go
  - Sends GateSignal payloads to /internal/gates/v1/signals (auth via service token + request id header).
  - Payload includes: decision (pass/fail/insufficient_data), reason, observed metrics, baseline metrics, window, and tags.
- Configuration: adds OPTIMIZATION_SAFEGUARD_* env vars (window, warmup, thresholds, min sample sizes, dry-run toggle).

Illustrative payload (log excerpt)
GateSignal{ gate="optimization.latency_regression", stage_id="stg_01J...", scope="tenant=t_8d3 model=llama3.1-70b region=us-east-1", config_version="ocv_1742", decision="fail", reason="p95 +18.4% (+42ms) vs baseline ocv_1739", metrics="p95=270ms p99=690ms 5xx=0.22%", baseline="p95=228ms p99=610ms 5xx=0.07%", window="10m", warmup="3m" }

Files / areas changed (high level)
- cmd/optimization_safeguard_canary/main.go (new)
- internal/metrics/client.go (adds query timeout + request-scoped logger)
- internal/metrics/queries/optimization_rollout.go (new)
- internal/gates/publisher.go (new)
- internal/gates/types.go (adds GateSignalDecision=insufficient_data)
- deploy/cronjobs/optimization-safeguard.yaml (new)
- config/env.sample (new vars + docs)
- tests: internal/metrics/queries/optimization_rollout_test.go, internal/gates/publisher_test.go

Why perf-canary (vs control plane)
We want this logic to be operationally owned by SRE/Obs and to share the same query/patterns as other perf canaries. Control-plane gating stays lightweight: it consumes standardized gate signals.

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - baseline selection (previous stable vs fallback)
  - warmup skip behavior
  - min sample size -> insufficient_data
  - regression decision logic and threshold overrides
  - publisher idempotency keying
- Local smoke test:
  - DRY_RUN=1 OPTIMIZATION_SAFEGUARD_TARGET=staging
  - Verified log output and query shapes against staging Prometheus.

Operational notes
- Default is “dry-run” in staging and “publish enabled” behind a feature flag in prod (PERF_CANARY_OPTIMIZATION_SAFEGUARD_ENABLED).
- Alerts: not added here; this job is intended to feed rollout gating. Follow-up PR will add an alert on repeated insufficient_data and on publisher errors.

Rollout plan
1) Merge + deploy to staging with dry-run; validate false positive rate during internal beta rollouts.
2) Enable publishing for internal accounts only (feature flag + allowlist of tenant IDs).
3) Expand to limited GA accounts.

Review conversation (highlights)
- Sean Gallagher: “We need explicit handling for cache invalidation warmup; otherwise this will page us all week.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added warmup skip + minimum sample size requirement, and included ‘insufficient_data’ decision so the controller can pause rather than fail.
- Allison Grant: “Please ensure metrics are tagged with config_version everywhere; otherwise the baseline/canary split is brittle.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added an explicit guard that errors if config_version label is missing from query results and logs a structured warning with the rollout scope.
- Benji Okafor: “Gate ingest endpoint requires idempotency; we can’t store an unbounded stream.”
  - Aisha Bello: Implemented deterministic idempotency_key = sha256(stage_id + tenant + model + region + gate_name).
- Sofia Ivanova: “Latency % alone is tricky at low baselines; add absolute ms bounds.”
  - Aisha Bello: Updated thresholds to require both relative and absolute deltas.

CI
- build: pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- docker image build: pass

Merge
Merged via squash into main.

Follow-ups
- Add a small library wrapper so control-plane and perf-canary share the same gate rule names/strings.
- Add explicit alerting on publisher errors and ‘insufficient_data’ rates by region.
- Add a “partial rollout” mode to compare canary shards vs non-canary shards within the same config_version when baseline is unavailable.

title: Emit structured audit log events for Optimization Config lifecycle (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback) + schema updates
preview: Context: Optimization config changes (batching/caching/quantization profiles) are production-impacting and must be traceable and reviewable. We’ve had gaps reported by enterprise customers (SUP-18544) and internal operators (INT-3390) where rollback actions were not consistently visible in audit exports, and some lifecycle steps were only logged in app logs. This PR standardizes and emits structured audit events for the full Optimization Config lifecycle and updates the audit schema registry accordingly. What’s included: (1) New/standardized event names under the "optimization_config" namespace: optimization_config.version_created, optimization_config.version_validated, optimization_config.apply_requested, optimization_config.rollout_started, optimization_config.rollout_paused, optimization_config.rollout_resumed, optimization_config.rollout_step_completed, optimization_config.rollout_failed, optimization_config.version_promoted, optimization_config.rollback_requested, optimization_config.rollback_completed, optimization_config.rollback_failed. (2) Consistent required fields across events: event_id, occurred_at, actor_type (user|service), actor_id, actor_email (when actor_type=user), actor_roles (best-effort snapshot), source (console|api|controller), request_id, trace_id (if present), tenant_id, scope (tenant/model/region; serialized as strings), optimization_config_id, optimization_config_version_id, from_version_id/to_version_id (when applicable), rollout_id (for staged apply), canary_percent (when applicable), reason (free-form operator reason; required for rollback_requested), gate_summary (serialized compact string: "latency_p95:pass;5xx:fail" etc.), eval_run_id (only for quantization-related applies where eval gating was invoked), and outcome (success|failure) with error_code/error_message when failure. (3) Explicit PII/secret handling: the audit payload does NOT include raw config bodies, prompt data, or customer inputs; the only config-related identifiers are config_id/version_id and a small "diff_fingerprint" (sha256 of normalized diff) for correlation. (4) Event emission points: service-layer emission in Optimization Config Service for create/validate/apply/promote/rollback API endpoints; rollout controller emits step/progression and gate evaluation results; rollback controller emits completion/failure. (5) Schema updates: updated audit schema registry entries for all new events, plus tightened validation rules for required fields and enums; added forward-compatible optional fields for private deployments (cluster_id, deployment_id) without making them required. (6) Backfill/compat: kept prior legacy event names as aliases for 30 days (emitting both) behind feature flag AUDIT_OPT_CONFIG_DUAL_EMIT; Console and downstream export pipelines continue to function. Example payload (redacted): {"event_name":"optimization_config.rollback_requested","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"u_7f...","actor_email":"oncall@redwoodinference.com","source":"console","tenant_id":"t_123","scope":"model=llama-3.1-70b;region=us-east-1","optimization_config_id":"ocfg_91...","optimization_config_version_id":"v_42","from_version_id":"v_43","to_version_id":"v_42","reason":"p95 regression during canary","request_id":"req_...","trace_id":"tr_..."}. Testing/verification: unit tests for event construction and schema validation; integration tests asserting audit emission on each endpoint; controller tests verifying pause/resume and failure paths emit the right events; manual verification in staging that events appear in SIEM-export sink with correct field names. Rollout plan: ship behind AUDIT_OPT_CONFIG_EVENTS_V1 (default off); enable for internal beta tenants first; after 1 week with no schema errors in export, disable dual-emit and remove aliases in follow-up PR. Checklist: [x] event taxonomy reviewed with Security (Marcus) [x] fields aligned with RBAC expectations (Jordan) [x] no raw config bodies logged [x] added docs snippet to internal audit-event catalog [x] added dashboard annotation hooks is tracked separately (observability-pack PR).

What’s included: (1) New/standardized event names under the "optimization_config" namespace: optimization_config.version_created, optimization_config.version_validated, optimization_config.apply_requested, optimization_config.rollout_started, optimization_config.rollout_paused, optimization_config.rollout_resumed, optimization_config.rollout_step_completed, optimization_config.rollout_failed, optimization_config.version_promoted, optimization_config.rollback_requested, optimization_config.rollback_completed, optimization_config.rollback_failed. (2) Consistent required fields across events: event_id, occurred_at, actor_type (user|service), actor_id, actor_email (when actor_type=user), actor_roles (best-effort snapshot), source (console|api|controller), request_id, trace_id (if present), tenant_id, scope (tenant/model/region; serialized as strings), optimization_config_id, optimization_config_version_id, from_version_id/to_version_id (when applicable), rollout_id (for staged apply), canary_percent (when applicable), reason (free-form operator reason; required for rollback_requested), gate_summary (serialized compact string: "latency_p95:pass;5xx:fail" etc.), eval_run_id (only for quantization-related applies where eval gating was invoked), and outcome (success|failure) with error_code/error_message when failure. (3) Explicit PII/secret handling: the audit payload does NOT include raw config bodies, prompt data, or customer inputs; the only config-related identifiers are config_id/version_id and a small "diff_fingerprint" (sha256 of normalized diff) for correlation. (4) Event emission points: service-layer emission in Optimization Config Service for create/validate/apply/promote/rollback API endpoints; rollout controller emits step/progression and gate evaluation results; rollback controller emits completion/failure. (5) Schema updates: updated audit schema registry entries for all new events, plus tightened validation rules for required fields and enums; added forward-compatible optional fields for private deployments (cluster_id, deployment_id) without making them required. (6) Backfill/compat: kept prior legacy event names as aliases for 30 days (emitting both) behind feature flag AUDIT_OPT_CONFIG_DUAL_EMIT; Console and downstream export pipelines continue to function. Example payload (redacted): {"event_name":"optimization_config.rollback_requested","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"u_7f...","actor_email":"oncall@redwoodinference.com","source":"console","tenant_id":"t_123","scope":"model=llama-3.1-70b;region=us-east-1","optimization_config_id":"ocfg_91...","optimization_config_version_id":"v_42","from_version_id":"v_43","to_version_id":"v_42","reason":"p95 regression during canary","request_id":"req_...","trace_id":"tr_..."}. Testing/verification: unit tests for event construction and schema validation; integration tests asserting audit emission on each endpoint; controller tests verifying pause/resume and failure paths emit the right events; manual verification in staging that events appear in SIEM-export sink with correct field names. Rollout plan: ship behind AUDIT_OPT_CONFIG_EVENTS_V1 (default off); enable for internal beta tenants first; after 1 week with no schema errors in export, disable dual-emit and remove aliases in follow-up PR. Checklist: [x] event taxonomy reviewed with Security (Marcus) [x] fields aligned with RBAC expectations (Jordan) [x] no raw config bodies logged [x] added docs snippet to internal audit-event catalog [x] added dashboard annotation hooks is tracked separately (observability-pack PR).

title: Add quality gate state machine + persistence for Optimize config changes
preview: This PR introduces the Quality Gate Run state machine and persistence layer in the Optimize service. Optimize recommendations (batching/caching/quantization profile changes) will be required to attach a gate run before they can be applied to production, but this PR only implements the core data model + transition logic + initial API endpoints (wiring to eval-harness and Console happens in follow-ups). The state machine is intentionally strict and idempotent to support retries, partial failures, and eventual coupling to rollout promotion. Includes DB migrations, internal admin endpoints, and notes for backfilling existing recommendations with `gate_required=false` until policy enforcement lands.

title: runtime/scheduler: add queue time, batch assembly, and batch size distribution metrics
preview:
Title: runtime/scheduler: add queue time, batch assembly, and batch size distribution metrics

Context
Perf baseline & representative workload suite work (Q1 2026) needs better attribution for TTFT/TPOT regressions: we can currently see end-to-end latency and some GPU-level counters, but we don’t have enough scheduler-level detail to answer: is p99 worse because requests wait longer in the queue, because batch formation takes longer, or because we’re forming suboptimal batches?

This PR adds low-overhead scheduler instrumentation for:
- Queue wait time (enqueue -> dequeued into a batch)
- Batch assembly time (start building batch -> submit to execution)
- Batch size distributions and “why” counters (e.g. flush reason)

These metrics are used by benchmark-lab/perf-suite runs and will be consumed by baseline dashboards + perf-canary gating. In production, the default configuration is conservative (sampling + coarse histograms + no per-request labels).

What’s included
1) New metrics (runtime)
All metrics are emitted from the scheduler loop on the server side. Definitions:
- redwood_scheduler_queue_wait_ms (histogram)
  - Measures time a request spent waiting in the scheduler’s pending queue before being admitted into a batch.
  - Scope: per request, measured at scheduler boundary (server-side only).
- redwood_scheduler_batch_assembly_ms (histogram)
  - Measures wall time spent assembling a batch (collecting requests, checking constraints, building the submission payload).
  - Scope: per batch.
- redwood_scheduler_batch_size (histogram)
  - Number of requests per submitted batch.
- redwood_scheduler_batch_tokens_prefill (histogram)
  - Total prefill tokens in the batch at submit time.
- redwood_scheduler_batch_tokens_decode (histogram)
  - Total decode tokens “budget” at submit time (best-effort estimate; see notes below).
- redwood_scheduler_flush_reason_total (counter)
  - Reasons: max_batch_size, max_tokens, max_queue_delay, no_capacity, fairness_yield, model_mismatch.
- redwood_scheduler_overload_dropped_total (counter)
  - Incremented when scheduler load-sheds due to overload/backpressure thresholds (existing behavior; this makes it visible).

Tagging (cardinality guardrails)
- Required tags (always on): model_family, deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private), region, gpu_sku
- Optional tags (off by default; on for perf-suite runs): bench_run_id, workload_name
- Explicitly NOT tagging by: request_id, tenant/customer_id, route, prompt_length_bin (those are handled elsewhere, or in benchmark harness results)

2) Feature flags / guardrails
- Default: only batch-level metrics on; request-level queue-wait metrics sampled.
- New config:
  - scheduler.metrics.enabled (default true)
  - scheduler.metrics.request_sample_rate (default 0.01 in prod, 1.0 in benchmark mode)
  - scheduler.metrics.enable_bench_tags (default false)
  - scheduler.metrics.histogram_mode (coarse|fine; default coarse)
- In benchmark mode (env REDWOOD_BENCHMARK_MODE=1), the server:
  - sets request_sample_rate=1.0
  - enables bench tags
  - switches histograms to “fine” bucket set

3) Minimal codepath overhead
- No allocations in the hot loop for the common case (coarse histograms + no extra tags).
- Queue-wait timing uses an integer timestamp stored on the Request object at enqueue time; sampled emission avoids per-request histogram updates at 100% QPS.
- Batch assembly timer uses a single timer per batch.

Notes / measurement boundaries
- Queue wait starts at scheduler enqueue and ends at admission into a batch. It does not include network receive, request parsing, auth, or model execution.
- Batch assembly time includes time spent in scheduler checks and building the batch payload, but not time waiting for GPU execution.
- Decode tokens at batch submit time are an estimate derived from request max_tokens and current phase; it is intended for distribution/trend analysis (not accounting).

Illustrative snippet (for reviewers)
- Request enqueue timestamp:
  - request->scheduler_enqueued_at_ns = clock::now_ns();
- Sampled queue wait:
  - if (rng.sample(request_sample_rate)) histogram.observe(now - request->scheduler_enqueued_at_ns)
- Batch assembly:
  - auto t0 = clock::now_ns();
  - ... assemble ...
  - batch_assembly.observe(clock::now_ns() - t0)

Files / areas changed (high level)
- runtime/scheduler/
  - added enqueue timestamp plumbing
  - batch assembly timer + flush reason counters
- runtime/metrics/
  - new metric definitions and bucket sets
- runtime/config/
  - scheduler.metrics.* config surface + benchmark-mode defaults
- docs/internal/
  - metric definitions + dashboard query examples

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - SchedulerMetricsTest.QueueWaitSamplingDoesNotEmitWhenDisabled
  - SchedulerMetricsTest.FlushReasonCounters
  - ConfigTest.BenchmarkModeOverrides
- Load test (local): verified CPU overhead within noise at 50k rps with request_sample_rate=0.01.
- Benchmark harness: ran representative chat streaming workload; metrics visible and correlate with observed TTFT changes when forcing max_queue_delay.

Rollout plan
- Merge behind conservative defaults.
- Enable bench tags only in benchmark fleet via REDWOOD_BENCHMARK_MODE.
- After dashboards land, SRE can choose to temporarily increase sampling for incident triage with a time-bounded config override.

Review conversation (abridged)
- Sean Gallagher (SRE): “Please ensure we’re not exploding cardinality in prod. bench_run_id/workload_name must be opt-in.”
  - Sofia: Updated config to default bench tags OFF; benchmark mode is explicit.
- Allison Grant (Observability): “Histogram buckets need to match our standards; avoid high-resolution buckets in prod.”
  - Sofia: Added histogram_mode (coarse|fine) and made coarse default.
- Noah Patel: “Queue-wait measurement boundary should be documented; otherwise people will misinterpret it as end-to-end latency.”
  - Sofia: Added ‘Notes / measurement boundaries’ section and inline comments in code.
- Marcus Reed: “Can we emit a batch size distribution split by prefill vs decode phase?”
  - Sofia: Added batch_tokens_prefill and batch_tokens_decode histograms; phase split for batch size itself deferred to follow-up to keep cardinality controlled.

CI
- Buildkite: runtime-build (pass)
- Unit tests: runtime-unit (pass)
- Lint/format: clang-format + bazel lint (pass)
- Perf smoke (non-gating): scheduler-metrics-smoke (pass)

Merge
- Squash merged to main.

Follow-ups
- Wire these metrics into baseline dashboards spec (separate PR in observability-pack).
- Add trace correlation hook for perf-suite runs (sampled) so dashboard -> trace drill-down is one click.

title: runtime: export scheduler queueing-time metrics + batch-size distribution
preview: Motivation: perf-canary is showing end-to-end latency regressions that are hard to attribute (kernel/runtime execution vs scheduler pressure). This PR exports scheduler queueing-time metrics and batch-size distribution at the runtime layer so we can: (1) split latency into queueing vs execution for canary attribution, (2) detect regressions caused by batching heuristics / admission control changes, (3) avoid relying on sampling-heavy trace-only approaches. What’s included: - New histogram metric for scheduler queueing time (enqueue -> execution start). - New histogram metric for batch size at execution start (effective batch after coalescing). - Structured dimension set aligned with telemetry cardinality constraints (no per-request identifiers). - Wiring to existing runtime metrics registry + export path. - Unit tests for metric emission + bounds (no negative durations, no zero/negative batch sizes). Metric definitions (names / units): - redwood_runtime_scheduler_queueing_time_ms (histogram, ms) - redwood_runtime_scheduler_batch_size (histogram, count) - redwood_runtime_scheduler_queueing_time_ms_count / _sum / _bucket (exported by histogram backend) Dimensions (labels): model (canonical name), model_version (registry version id), region, tier (hosted|dedicated|private), pool (logical serving pool), hardware_sku, runtime_build_id, scheduler_policy (coarse enum). Notes: - scheduler_policy is intentionally coarse ("classic" | "continuous" | "unknown") to avoid config-cardinality blowups. - We do NOT emit request_id, tenant_id, prompt_length, or any user identifiers. - Metrics are emitted at the point a request leaves the scheduler queue and is assigned to a GPU execution slot; for batched execution, queueing time is tracked per request, and batch_size is tracked per batch. - Backfill: none; this is new time series. Canary and dashboards can start querying once the runtime rolls out. Rollout plan: ship behind runtime flag RuntimeMetrics.SchedulerQueueing (default ON in staging, ON in prod after canary validates). If metrics volume is higher than expected, we can disable via the flag without runtime restart (dynamic config refresh). Validation: verified under synthetic load in staging (benchmark-lab runner) that queueing time rises with induced scheduler saturation and returns to baseline when load is removed; batch-size histogram shifts as expected when toggling continuous batching depth. Related: ENG-4875 adds scheduler-level instrumentation; perf-canary dashboards/alerts will follow in observability-pack. Screenshots: none (metrics-only).

title: Add schema + SIEM mappings for offline bundle verification audit events
preview:
Context: Redwood Private offline bundle signing/verifying is landing for air-gapped installs. The installer now emits local audit events for bundle verification outcomes (success + specific failure reasons). Customers who forward audit logs to a SIEM via audit-log-exporter need these events to be recognized, normalized, and exported in a stable schema.

What this PR does:
- Adds new event definitions to audit-log-exporter’s schema catalog for bundle verification outcomes.
- Adds normalization/mapping rules for JSON (native), CEF, and LEEF output modes.
- Ensures failure reasons are exported as a bounded enum + optional details field (redaction-aware).
- Updates unit tests + golden fixtures for all output formats.

New events (normalized):
- redwood.private.bundle_verification.succeeded
- redwood.private.bundle_verification.failed

Normalized fields:
- event.name (string)
- event.outcome (success|failure)
- event.time (RFC3339)
- redwood.bundle.id (string, build id / bundle build identifier)
- redwood.bundle.version (string, semantic version)
- redwood.bundle.manifest_sha256 (string)
- redwood.bundle.signature.key_id (string)
- redwood.bundle.signature.trust (trusted|untrusted|unknown)
- redwood.bundle.verify.failure_reason (enum; only when failure)
- redwood.bundle.verify.failure_detail (string; optional; redacted by default)
- host.name / cluster.id (when available in exporter input)

Failure reason enum (initial set):
- MANIFEST_HASH_MISMATCH
- ARTIFACT_HASH_MISMATCH
- SIGNATURE_INVALID
- SIGNING_KEY_UNTRUSTED
- SIGNING_KEY_UNKNOWN
- SIGNATURE_EXPIRED
- SIGNATURE_NOT_YET_VALID
- DOWNGRADE_BLOCKED
- BUNDLE_LAYOUT_INVALID
- VERIFICATION_SKIPPED_BREAK_GLASS

Notes:
- We intentionally avoid exporting file paths / artifact names by default (can leak customer topology); failure_detail is scrubbed unless operator sets `redaction.allow_sensitive=true`.
- CEF/LEEF mappings keep the set small (key_id, bundle_id, version, outcome, failure_reason). We add a single extension field for manifest_sha256.

Why now:
- Pilot customers are already asking why their SIEM doesn’t show verification events while local logs clearly do (see SUP-31910 for a post-rotation unknown key case).

Docs:
- Internal: aligns with the audit event schema review thread and the Private on-prem audit event definition.

Rollout:
- Backward compatible. If events are not present, exporter behavior unchanged. If present, they are recognized and included in the output stream.

Testing:
- Unit tests for schema registration
- Golden output tests for JSON/CEF/LEEF
- Fuzz test for failure_reason parsing (ensures unknown values map to OTHER and do not crash the pipeline)

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Add dashboards/queries snippets for Splunk + Sentinel (tracked in ENG-4850 enablement work)
- Add per-tenant routing rules for customers who want bundle verification events in a separate index

Screenshots/Examples:
- JSON output example and CEF mapping included in test fixtures + PR notes below.

Risk:
- Low runtime risk; schema-only change. Main risk is field naming drift; mitigated by golden fixtures and explicit enum list.

title: Implement chat streaming workload generator (request mix, concurrency ramp, token pacing) + TTFT/TPOT export
preview:
Motivation: We need a production-representative chat streaming workload for the Q1 2026 perf baseline & suite. Existing harness coverage is request/response (non-streaming) only, which under-measures scheduling + queueing dynamics and makes TTFT/TPOT comparisons noisy across client implementations.

What this adds: (1) A chat streaming workload generator (server SSE / chunked streaming) with configurable request-mix, sequence-length bins, and streaming cadence; (2) a concurrency ramp runner (warmup + stepped load) to mimic bursty traffic and reduce variance; (3) standardized baseline outputs for TTFT + TPOT with explicit measurement boundaries (client-side network excluded as much as practical).

Key behaviors: Request mix is modeled as weighted "cohorts" (e.g., short prompt + short decode, short prompt + long decode, medium prompt + medium decode) with configurable distributions. Concurrency ramps from N0 -> N1 -> ... with per-step dwell time, plus a warmup phase that primes model weights and KV allocations without counting metrics. Streaming pacing is implemented as a token-consumption loop that intentionally reads from the stream as fast as possible (to avoid client backpressure becoming the bottleneck). We also capture a separate "client_read_lag_ms" diagnostic to detect accidental client throttling.

Metrics: We compute TTFT as time from request send -> first token/chunk observed by client (first content token, not headers). TPOT is computed as (time from first token -> last token) / (num_tokens_after_first) and exported along with total tokens, completion time, and basic HTTP error classification. Output is emitted as JSONL (one line per request) plus an aggregated summary JSON with p50/p95/p99 for TTFT and TPOT, and throughput (req/s and output_tokens/s). The summary file is shaped to be ingested by the baseline dashboards and aligns with ADR-0172 metrics naming (full manifest wiring comes in PR-21432).

How to run: `bench run chat_streaming --endpoint $URL --model llama-3.1-70b --region us-east --mix mixes/chat_streaming_v1.json --ramp ramps/step_10min.json --seed 42 --out out/2026-01-05/`.

Notable implementation details: The generator supports both OpenAI-compatible chat completions streaming and Redwood API streaming, controlled via a `protocol` flag. We use a monotonic clock source for timing and include per-request attempt IDs for correlation with server traces. Error handling distinguishes connect timeouts, first-byte timeouts, mid-stream disconnects, and server 429/5xx to help triage overload vs regressions.

Testing: Adds unit tests for distribution sampling and pacing edge cases (empty stream, immediate EOS, chunk coalescing) and a small integration test that replays a canned streaming trace to verify TTFT/TPOT computations are stable.

Follow-ups: (a) Replace the temporary summary schema with the canonical run manifest + results format once PR-21432 lands; (b) add long-prefill workload (ENG-4824) and embeddings mode (ENG-4825); (c) wire the ramp runner into perf-canary nightly after threshold calibration (ENG-4828).

title: runtime: make optimization config applier idempotent + retry-safe (partial rollout safeguards)
preview:
Context: As part of Safe apply + rollback for Optimization Configs, the control plane will issue apply operations that may be retried (network timeouts, controller restarts) and can be delivered out-of-order relative to worker restarts and shard movement. Prior implementation applied configs as a best-effort "set current" and would re-run side effects on every delivery (cache invalidation, batcher reinit), which increased tail latency and could leave mixed worker states during partial rollout. This PR makes the runtime Optimization Config applier idempotent and resilient to retries, and adds explicit safeguards for partial rollout states.

What changed: 1) Introduced apply_operation_id and deterministic apply keying. The runtime now consumes ApplyOptimizationConfig(scope, version, apply_operation_id, reason) and records a durable ApplyRecord per (scope, version). If the same apply_operation_id is delivered again, the operation is treated as a no-op after verifying the recorded checksum. 2) Added a small durable state store (RuntimeOptimizationConfigState) to persist last_desired_version, last_applied_version, last_apply_operation_id, and an apply_status enum (PENDING/APPLIED/FAILED). This survives worker restarts and prevents "double apply" side effects after process crashes. 3) Side-effect gating: cache invalidation and batch scheduler resets now run only on transition from one applied version to another, and are skipped on idempotent retries. 4) Partial rollout safeguards: if a worker receives an apply for an older version than its recorded desired_version, it will reject it with a conflict response unless reason=ROLLBACK (explicit rollback) or allow_downgrade=true from control plane. This prevents late/stale apply messages from reintroducing a regressed version mid-rollout. 5) Repair behavior: if apply_status is FAILED for the same (scope, version) and the controller retries with a new apply_operation_id, the runtime will re-attempt apply (previously a failed apply could wedge workers until manual restart). 6) Observability: added metrics and structured logs to make retry/no-op/conflict behavior visible: optimization_config_apply_total{result=applied|noop|conflict|failed}, optimization_config_apply_duration_ms, and optimization_config_state_gauge{state}. All are tagged with tenant/model/region/config_version/apply_operation_id (apply_operation_id only in logs to avoid high cardinality metrics).

Why this is safe: The new behavior is strictly more conservative. Duplicate deliveries become no-ops, stale messages are rejected, and only explicit rollbacks can downgrade. Persisted state ensures crashes don’t cause reapplication storms (and the associated cache flush latency spikes). Unit + integration tests cover retry ordering, crash/restart, and conflict semantics.

Follow-ups: - Control plane rollout controller will start supplying apply_operation_id everywhere (tracked in ENG-4824). - Console will surface conflict/failed reasons using the error codes introduced here. - We should consider promoting apply_operation_id into trace spans once tracing propagation is in place for the apply pipeline.

Related: ENG-4823 (idempotency guarantees), PR #19915 (apply transaction checkpoints), PR #19933 (gate hooks/metric tags).
